General safety information
Important safety instructions
1.1 Important safety instructions
It is important to install, operate, maintain, and dispose of the equipment according to the
information in the product documentation, and in compliance with local regulations. For the
safety of the users and the patients, before a user operates or works on the equipment, they
must read, understand, and obey all:
• Warnings, cautions, instructions, and notes in this document and the related
documents.
• Safety instructions, emergency procedures, and information in this document and the
related documents.
• Warnings, cautions, and safety markings on the equipment and the accessories of the
equipment.
If you have local work instructions for the equipment, then Elekta recommends that you include
the warnings, cautions, and notes from this document in your local work instructions.
1.1.1 Conventions for warnings, cautions, and notes
The conventions give information about the class and the level of the risk in the warnings and
cautions, and the content of notes.
WARNING 1.1
A warning is information about procedures, tasks, or conditions with a risk of fatal or serious
injury, injury, or clinical mistreatment, if you do not obey the instructions.
CAUTION 1.1
A caution is information about procedures, tasks, or conditions with a risk of damage to the
equipment, data, or the environment, if you do not obey the instructions.
Note: A note gives more information about a related text.
1.1.2 Expected service life of the equipment
The expected service life is the length of time that the regular service and repair procedures can
keep the equipment safe to operate.
WARNING 1.2
Do not operate the equipment unless you correctly complete the safety checks, planned
maintenance schedules, and extended life checks. If you ignore this warning, you can cause
fatal injury and damage to the equipment.
The expected service life of this equipment is 15 years of normal use.
The Elekta definition for 'expected service life' is the length of time that the regular service and
repair procedures can keep the equipment safe to operate.
The Elekta definition of 'normal use' is the treatment of 50 patients each day for five days each
week of the year. If the equipment is used more frequently than this, the frequency of the
planned maintenance must increase accordingly and the extended life checks must also be
brought forward accordingly.
XVI R5.0x Document ID: 1522605_06
Corrective Maintenance Manual
© Elekta Group 2021. All rights reserved. Page 7

General safety information
Important safety instructions
The level of operation of the equipment can be different to ‘normal use’. This has an effect on the
length of the service life. Because of wear of parts, the risk of a malfunction can increase after 10
years operation. Elekta recommends that before you continue operation after 10 years, you
complete the regular extended life checks.
Elekta can help the User with the correct safety checks, planned maintenance schedules, and the
regular extended life checks for continued safe operation of the equipment.
The system must be maintained throughout its expected service life in compliance with the EMC
information detailed in the applicable Instructions for Use.
For information on the above checks and schedules, contact your local Elekta representative.

of, and work on, the equipment.

1.2 Safety precautions
1.2.1 Safety precautions for the compatibility of the equipment
WARNING 1.3
Do not use parts, accessories, or other equipment, without approval by Elekta of their
compatibility. If you ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury and damage to the
equipment.
Only operate the equipment with Elekta supplied or approved, compatible equipment or parts.
Accessories, transducers, and cables that are not specified by or do not have the approval of
Elekta can cause an incorrect treatment delivery. They can also have an effect on the performance
of the electromagnetic compatibility (EMC), which can increase the emissions or decrease the
immunity of the equipment.
Contact Elekta for information about the compatibility of other equipment or parts.
1.2.2 Safety precautions for the electromagnetic compatibility
The design and manufacture of Elekta products is in compliance with applicable EMC standards.
The intended result of the standards is to minimize the risk of electromagnetic interference by RF
devices. The type of device and the distance to the equipment has an effect on the intensity of
the electromagnetic interference. The equipment includes the linear accelerator and accessories,
the control cabinets, the computers in the control room, and equipment from other
manufacturer.
Document ID: 1522605_06 XVI R5.0x
Corrective Maintenance Manual
Page 10 © Elekta Group 2021. All rights reserved.

General safety information
Safety precautions
It is necessary to have special precautions for EMC emissions. Elekta recommends that you obey
these precautions when you use RF devices near the equipment.
• Do not use RF devices unless they are in compliance with international standards for
electromagnetic compatibility, for example, the CE or FCC mark.
• Do not use a two-way emergency radio in the treatment room or the control room.
Only use an emergency radio if there is an emergency.
• Do not use cellular telephones, or other devices with cellular modems near the
equipment. The Instructions for Use contains information on the minimum safe
distance of these devices from the equipment.
• When you use a device that is enabled for wireless operation, it must be a minimum of
10 cm from the equipment.
• Radio-Frequency Identification (RFID) readers, when activated to do a scan of a RFID
tag, must be a minimum of one meter from the equipment.
Note: The RFID systems that Elekta supply use lower power levels. You can safely use these RFID systems
when you obey the procedures in the Instructions for Use for the product.
The system must be installed, put into service, and maintained in compliance with the EMC
information in the Instructions for Use.
Contact Elekta for information about the compatibility of parts and equipment.
WARNING 1.4
Do not put portable or mobile radio frequency (RF) communication devices near to the
equipment. The emissions from such RF devices can be more than the EMC standards, and can
have unwanted effects on the equipment operation. If you ignore this warning, the RF
emissions can cause the equipment to have a malfunction. A malfunction can cause fatal injury,
clinical mistreatment, or damage to the equipment.
WARNING 1.5
Do not use accessories, transducers, and cables that are not specified by Elekta. They can have
an effect on the EMC performance, which can increase EMC emissions or decrease the
immunity to RF interference of the equipment. If you ignore this warning, incorrect radiation
delivery and other malfunctions can occur, which can cause fatal injury or clinical mistreatment.
1.2.3 Safety precaution for Service Tools and lifting equipment
WARNING 1.6
Do not use defective or damaged lifting equipment. If you ignore this warning, you can cause
fatal injury and damage to the equipment.
WARNING 1.7
Do not use incorrectly calibrated or defective Service Tools. If you ignore this warning, you can
cause injury and damage to the equipment.
Before you use the Service Tool or lifting equipment, make sure that:
• There are no defective or damaged parts
• The calibration of the Service Tool is correct
XVI R5.0x Document ID: 1522605_06
Corrective Maintenance Manual
© Elekta Group 2021. All rights reserved. Page 11

General safety information
Safety precautions
• The applicable calibration certificate is valid
• The lifting equipment gives the maximum Safe Working Load (SWL).
For information on the maintenance and calibration schedules of the Service Tool, see the
Instructions for Use and Calibration Certificate for the tool.
1.2.4 Safety precaution for work with electrical and mechanical parts
WARNING 1.8
Do not connect any electrical power supply before the ground earth connections are secure.
There is a risk of high leakage current. If you ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury.
WARNING 1.9
Do not remove the cables or the covers, or operate the equipment without the covers. If you
ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury
The covers on the equipment prevent access to high voltage parts and parts that move. If you
remove the covers, there is a risk of high voltage shock or injury from parts that move.
Only remove the covers if you are an Elekta approved person with the correct authority and you
obey the applicable safety procedures.
1.2.5 Safety precaution for work with safety devices during service
operation
WARNING 1.10
Do not remove, change, or override the safety switches, interlocks, and other safety devices. If
you ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury or clinical mistreatment.
The procedures in this document can tell you to remove, change, or override the safety switches,
interlocks, or other safety devices during repair or maintenance work. When the work is
completed, make all the safety devices active again, and do a test of them.
1.2.6 Safety precaution for work on BLDs with UV lighting
WARNING 1.11
Do not look into the ultraviolet lighting of the BLD without protective glasses. If you ignore this
warning, you can cause serious injury.
The covers and screens of the BLD prevent an injury to your eyes from the ultraviolet lighting.
Before you remove the covers or screens of the BLD, switch off the linear accelerator, or use
protective glasses. Protective glasses must have the UV-A specification OD 6+ @ 190 - 532 Nm.
If there is damage to the covers or screen of the BLD, switch off the linear accelerator and contact
the Elekta representative.
Document ID: 1522605_06 XVI R5.0x
Corrective Maintenance Manual
Page 12 © Elekta Group 2021. All rights reserved.

General safety information
Safety precautions
1.2.7 Safety precaution for work with lasers
WARNING 1.12
Do not look into the laser beams because they can cause damage to your eyes. If you ignore
this warning, you can cause serious injury.
Elekta systems use the laser beams to help set up patients in the correct position for treatment.
During your work in the treatment room, make sure that you do not look into the laser beams.
1.2.8 Safety precautions for work with display screen equipment
WARNING 1.13
Do not use the display screen equipment for long periods of time. If you ignore this warning,
you can cause injury.
When you use the display screen equipment (DSE), obey the applicable Health and Safety
regulations and procedures in the jurisdiction where you do the work.
1.2.9 Safety precautions for cleaning and disinfection
WARNING 1.14
Do not clean the equipment, or use a disinfectant spray when the equipment is connected to
the electrical supply. If you ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury.
WARNING 1.15
Do not use a spray that is explosive or flammable. If you ignore this warning, you can cause
fatal injury and damage to the equipment.
CAUTION 1.2
Do not let water and other liquids go into the equipment. If you ignore this caution, you can
cause damage to the equipment.
CAUTION 1.3
Do not use a spray to clean the medical equipment room. If you ignore this caution, you can
cause damage to the equipment.
At regular intervals, it is necessary to clean the equipment and equipment room with
disinfectants. Elekta recommends that the procedures to clean the equipment and the equipment
room obey the applicable laws and regulations of the jurisdiction where the equipment is
installed.
XVI R5.0x Document ID: 1522605_06
Corrective Maintenance Manual
© Elekta Group 2021. All rights reserved. Page 13

General safety information
Safety precautions
1.2.10 Safety precautions for work with high temperatures on the BLD
covers
During normal operation, the maximum temperatures of the accessory ring and BLD outer covers
can increase to 45 °C. Make sure that you do not touch the outer covers or the accessory ring for
more than 1 minute.
1.2.11 Safety precaution for the installation of accessories on the BLD
WARNING 1.16
Do not touch the connector found in the accessory ring on the BLD. If you ignore this warning,
you can cause injury.
When you install an accessory, for example, a shadow tray, electron applicator, or front pointer,
do not touch the pins in the connector. The pins have a low electrical current, which can give an
electric shock when you touch them.
1.2.12 Safety precautions for work with heavy parts
The safety precautions for work with heavy parts are the necessary safety precautions to know to
prevent injury.
WARNING 1.17
Do not lift or move heavy parts unless you obey the applicable Health and Safety regulations
and procedures. If you ignore this warning, you can cause serious injury.
1.2.13 Safety precautions during work on the BLD
When you do work on the BLD, make sure that you obey the following safety precautions:
WARNING 1.18
Do not do work on the inner parts of the beam limiting device while the electrical supply is
connected. There is a risk of high voltage electric shock. If you ignore this warning, you can
cause fatal injury.
WARNING 1.19
Do not do work on the beam limiting device unless the gantry is at 0° or 180°. Gravity can cause
the leaves, diaphragms, and dynamic leaf guides to move. If you ignore this warning, you can
cause fatal injury.
WARNING 1.20
Do not touch the hot LED field light projector, or the heat sink, immediately after operation. If
you ignore this warning, you can cause serious injury.
Document ID: 1522605_06 XVI R5.0x
Corrective Maintenance Manual
Page 14 © Elekta Group 2021. All rights reserved.

General safety information
Safety precautions
1.2.14 Safety precaution for the removal of parts from the radiation head
WARNING 1.21
Do not touch the flight tube, bending magnet, or other parts near to them until a check for
induced radioactivity is completed. Excessive exposure to radioactivity is dangerous. If you
ignore this warning, you can cause serious injury.
After the operation of the linear accelerator at high energy, the parts in the radiation head can
become radioactive and a radiation hazard. Before you do work on, or remove parts from the
radiation head, use a calibrated radiation detector to measure the level of induced radioactivity.
Use the applicable safety procedures when you remove radioactive parts and material. The
maximum dose rate for the safe removal of radioactive parts and material is 50 µSv/hr
(microsievert per hour). Do not remove the radioactive parts and material from the treatment
room before they are correctly prepared for safe removal.
1.2.15 Safety precautions for maintenance, modification, and
decommissioning
WARNING 1.22
Do not do work, or make modifications to the equipment without the correct training and
authority. If you ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury, clinical mistreatment, and
damage to the equipment.
Only do work on, or make modifications to, the equipment if you are an Elekta approved person
with the applicable authority. Such work must use best practice and obey the laws and
regulations in the jurisdiction in which the equipment is installed.
Modifications or work that does not have approval by Elekta, and is not done by an Elekta
approved person with the correct authority, can cause:
• Fatal injury
• Incorrect radiation delivery
• Malfunction of the equipment
• Damage to the equipment
• The warranty to be canceled.
1.2.16 Safety precautions for work in areas of induced radioactivity
The safety precautions for work in areas of induced radioactivity are the precautions to prevent
an excessive exposure to, and serious injury from, induced radioactivity.
When you operate or do work on the equipment, Elekta recommends that you use the applicable
radiation protection functions, devices, systems, procedures, and accessories. Always obey the
applicable laws and regulations for induced radioactivity for the jurisdiction in which the
equipment is installed.
The safety precautions are applicable to:
• Maintenance, modifications, and decommissioning
• Measurement of the induced radioactivity
• Removal and movement of parts with induced radioactivity.
XVI R5.0x Document ID: 1522605_06
Corrective Maintenance Manual
© Elekta Group 2021. All rights reserved. Page 15

General safety information
Safety precautions
1.2.16.1 Safety precautions for the measurement of induced radioactivity
The safety precautions for the measurement of induced radioactivity are the necessary safety
precautions to know to prevent excessive exposure to radioactivity, which can cause serious
injury or damage to the environment.
Always use two radiation detectors to measure the level of induced radioactivity. Only use
radiation detectors that have an applicable calibration certificate. The period of time since the
last calibration date must be less than 12 months. Do not use the radiation detectors if the period
of time since the last calibration date is more than 12 months.
WARNING 1.23
Do not move radioactive material (by air, sea, or land) unless you obey the applicable laws and
regulations for induced radioactivity and disposal of radioactive material of the jurisdictions
through which the movement occurs. If you ignore this warning, you can cause serious injury
and damage to the environment.
1.2.16.2 Safety precautions for handling parts with induced radioactivity
The handling of parts with induced radioactivity must comply with the laws and regulations of the
jurisdiction in which the equipment is installed. The safe levels of radiation applicable in the UK
do not apply in all jurisdictions. The permitted level of radioactivity must always be as low as
reasonably achievable (ALARA). This level may vary depending on local rules, therefore the safe
working levels from the applicable local rules must be considered as the acceptable working limit.
If there are no safe levels available for a jurisdiction, use the UK value as a guideline, which is a
maximum of 150 cps at the surface for a maximum duration of 72 minutes. The following table
shows the conversion from this value to other units.
Table 1.2 Conversion between the units of induced radioactivity
Counts per second Microsieverts per Milliroentgen per
(cps) hour (µSv/hr) hour (mR/hr)
150 50 5
For information on the safe removal of parts with induced radioactivity and the safe levels of
radiation for your jurisdiction, contact Elekta or a Qualified Expert (QE).
1.3 Emergencies
1.3.1 Safety precaution for the occurrence of fire or explosion
WARNING 1.24
Do not use the equipment in rooms that contain explosive or flammable gases. If you ignore
this warning, you can cause fatal injury and damage to the equipment.
High levels of oxygen, and some anesthetic gases and disinfectant sprays, can make an explosive
or flammable environment in the room.
Document ID: 1522605_06 XVI R5.0x
Corrective Maintenance Manual
Page 16 © Elekta Group 2021. All rights reserved.

General safety information
Emergencies
1.3.2 Recommendations for emergency procedures
Before you use the equipment, Elekta recommends that you know all the local emergency
procedures and the locations of all the emergency switches.
The Elekta equipment includes:
• The STOP MOTORS buttons on the handheld controllers and user interface panels in
the treatment room, and FKP in the control room.
When you press these buttons, it disconnects the electrical supply from the motors,
stops all movements of the equipment, and stops kV and MV radiation.
• The EMERGENCY POWER OFF buttons in the treatment room, control area, and areas
near the equipment.
When you operate these buttons, it disconnects the electrical supply to the linear
accelerator. The buttons disconnect the electrical supply from many electrical circuits,
but not from all the circuits.
XVI R5.0x Document ID: 1522605_06

2.1.2 Recommendations for training
The regulations for training give a description of the necessary training, regulations, and
compliance that apply to the operation of the equipment.
Different countries have different regulations for training. Make sure that you have the necessary
training before you operate or do work on the equipment. Make sure that your training is in
compliance with the laws and regulations of the jurisdiction in which the equipment is installed.
Training is available from Elekta. Contact Elekta for more information about the applicable
training courses.
2.1.3 Disposal of the equipment
End of life (EOL) disposal is the procedure to disassemble, remove, and discard the equipment, in
full or in parts, to a condition where it is not possible to operate the equipment for its intended
use.
Only a Qualified Expert (QE) and an approved facility with an applicable license, may remove and
discard the equipment and recycle the material.
A full description of disposal requirements is detailed in a separate document Product Disposal
Information. This document is available on request from Elekta.
The customer owns the data on the storage media. If there are any data storage media such as
HDDs, remove them from the device and hand them to the customer for disposal. If the customer
entrusts the disposal team to dispose of the data, the data must be destroyed in accordance with
relevant cybersecurity regulations.

Regulatory requirements for equipment use
CAUTION 2.2
Do not touch the parts identified with the ESD caution label. Always obey the ESD precautions
during work on these parts. If you ignore this caution, you can cause damage to the equipment.
Semiconductor devices, integrated circuits, and the parts that contain them, are ESD sensitive
devices. These devices are electrostatic discharge sensitive (ESDS) devices. ESD can cause damage
to these devices and cause them to have a malfunction immediately or some time after the
electrostatic discharge. To prevent the ESD damage to these devices, always wear a grounded
antistatic wrist strap, and obey the applicable ESD precautions and procedures.
Elekta recommends that all users receive the training about ESD. The training must include the
ESD caution label and these ESD precautions.

Introduction
CMM Document scope
3.1 CMM Document scope
The corrective maintenance manual (CMM) helps service engineers troubleshoot, repair and
maintain the systems, subsystems and components of the linear accelerator. The target audience
is qualified service engineers. These may be engineers employed directly by Elekta, qualified
representatives of Elekta or qualified representatives of Elekta's customers.
The manual provides:
• A description of the system or sub-system in question.
• A troubleshooting section to identify the problem areas.
• A set of detailed procedures used to replace FRUs (field replaceable units).
• A section on how to return the device to work.
If you find any mistakes in this document, or identify areas for improvement, please raise a CLM
(customer lifecycle management) case or an ECR (Engineering change Request) and assign it to
the relevant queue.

Product Description
Description of XVI
4.1 Description of XVI
XVI together with a linear accelerator is an image guided radiation therapy (IGRT) system for the
delivery of prescribed doses of ionizing radiation, as approved by a licensed medical practitioner.
This is usually for the treatment of malignant neoplastic diseases.
WARNING 4.1
Do not use any other X-IGRT equipment without performing an evaluation of the correct
system operation and safety. If you ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury.
The XVI control system controls the kV generator, acquires images, and reconstructs volume
images from the acquired image sets.
Figure 4.1 Equipment for image guided radiation therapy in the treatment room (non-Harmony)
(1) kV source arm (2) MV detector panel
(3) kV detector panel (4) XVI handheld controller
XVI R5.0x Document ID: 1522605_06

About the XVI system

0016_LHPL
Figure 4.2 Equipment for image guided radiation therapy in the treatment room (Harmony)
(1) kV detector panel (2) kV source arm
(3) MV detector panel (4) Handheld controller (HHC)
XVI is an electronic imaging device for kV image acquisition. It gives more information to let you
set up the position of the patient more accurately for treatment. The intended use of the XVI
system does not include diagnostic operation.
In the treatment room, there is a kV source arm and a kV detector panel, which are part of the
XVI equipment. The kV source arm and the kV detector panel are opposite each other on the
gantry drum of the linear accelerator. It is necessary to extend the kV source arm and the kV
detector panel to acquire images with XVI. A kV generator is installed in the equipment room.
From the control room, you must manually start kV radiation with the XVI system and the
function keypad (FKP). When the kV detector panel is open and the kV source delivers X-rays, XVI
acquires images. For non-Harmony, there is a dedicated HHC for the XVI system in the treatment
room. For Harmony in the treatment room, there is an integrated HHC which can control the XVI
system. Use the HHC to control the position of the kV detector panel.
When the XVI system is not in operation, you can retract the kV source arm and move the kV
detector panel.
4.2 About the XVI system
The kV radiation subsystem produces the X-ray beam. It consists of the kV generator and the kV
source arm. The kV source arm contains an X-ray tube with primary filtration, and the housing for
collimator/filter cassettes that provide final (secondary) beam shaping. The kV source is installed
on a pair of support arms. For more information refer to sections
• kV generator
• kV source assembly

The kV imaging subsystem moves the kV detector panel to the necessary position and acquires
the planar or volume images of the patient. This subsystem consists of the kV detector arm, the
kV detector panel, a detector control board (DCB) and an arm control board (ACB). For more
information refer to sections
• kV detector arm
• kV detector panel
• Detector control board
• Arm control board.
The power supply subsystem provides conditioned DC electrical power for the kV detector arm.
For more information refer to section:
• Electrical power supplies.
The control subsystem gives users control of the XVI system. For more information refer to
section:
• XVI controls.

4.3 Principle of operation
When the XVI system is not in use, the digital accelerator operates in its normal clinical mode to
deliver radiotherapy to patients.
4.3.1 Software control
The XVI system is controlled by a software program running on the XVI control cabinet, where
parameters such as the timing and power characteristics of the X-ray pulses are configured. The
software is also used to examine and manipulate the acquired images.
4.3.2 Safety interlocks on the XVI system
The XVI system is connected to the interlock system of the host digital accelerator. The interlocks
prevent the emission of radiation by the kV generator if the system is in an unsafe condition, and
XVI R5.0x Document ID: 1522605_06
Corrective Maintenance Manual
© Elekta Group 2021. All rights reserved. Page 41

Product Description
XVI releases
immediately stop the emission of radiation if an unsafe condition occurs. This arrangement makes
sure that the XVI system operates safely by disabling the generation of X-rays unless the entire
system is in a safe condition.
The operation of an interlock that causes termination of the kV image acquisition results in an
aborted XVI scan. In this event, it is necessary to repeat the kV image acquisition cycle - exposing
the patient to an unplanned and unnecessary radiation dose. The interlock system of the digital
accelerator therefore operates only when essential to protect the patient and/or the user.
For detailed information on the interlock system of the host digital accelerator, refer to the
Corrective Maintenance Manual – Power Supplies and Interlocks.
4.4 XVI releases
kV imaging is controlled by the XVI software installed in the XVI control cabinet in the control
room. XVI is the standard software, which is installed on each system, the operating system is
Microsoft® Windows XP®, Microsoft® Windows Embedded (WES 7) and Microsoftt® Windows 10.
See below for description of all versions.
XVI Releases Description
XVI R5.0 XVI R5.0 includes:
• Operating system is Windows XP®
• Intra-fraction imaging
• Static into Volumetric imaging
• Annotation Overlay
• Improved integration with MOSAIQ®

4.5 Detector Readback Safety Kit (DRSK)
Note: The installation of the Detector Readback Safety Kit (DRSK) was mandatory and it is therefore
already installed on all systems.
Elekta released the Detector Readback Safety Kit (DRSK) modification kit because of a failure in
the position detection system. A defective potentiometer or slider switch on the kV Arm Control
Board (ACB), which can cause the incorrect position of the detector in the longitudinal or lateral
direction, would have caused such a failure.
An incorrect lateral position can have an effect on image registration, which can cause wasted
images and, as a result, wasted patient dose. More important, an incorrect longitudinal
potentiometer indication can cause incorrect interpretation of the patient position, and an error
in the subsequent table movement can cause mistreatment.
The DRSK modification kit included the installation of a new potentiometer assembly that
contains a second potentiometer wire. The first wire connects to the coarse and fine B
potentiometer, and the second wire connects to the fine A potentiometer. This configuration lets
the system detect changes in the phase relationship between the fine A and fine B
potentiometers to find a possible problem with one of the potentiometer wires.
The installation of the DRSK modification kit also included the installation of a breakout box that
separates the signal from the lateral and longitudinal potentiometer wires. This lets the extended
imaging module (XIM) change the analogue potentiometer indications into digital controller-area
network (CAN), to send to the XVI control system (PC) through the iViewGT™ CAN bus.
As part of the modification kit installation, an XVI software upgrade was also necessary. The XVI
software upgrade installed a new calibration tool that tells the software what the longitudinal and
lateral potentiometer positions are at each field of view (FOV). The software has inhibits that

software will cause inhibits in the conditions that follow:
The kV panel is in the incorrect FOV to the specified FOV in the preset.
For example:
If the kV panel is in the small FOV, when the preset is set to the medium FOV.
When the user moves the panel to the incorrect FOV and the panel is in an undefined position.
After a potentiometer or potentiometer wire failure, when the phase relationship between the
fine A and fine B potentiometer indications are not as specified, or the reference voltages are not
as specified.
4.6 kV generator - Sedecal SHF 435 DSI Series (non-applicable
for Harmony)
The Sedecal SHF 435 DSI Series generator is a 40 kW unit that can produce single and continuous
radiographic exposures of varying energy and dose.
When the foot switch or the kV Start button on the function key pad is pressed, the kV enable
signal to the kV generator is asserted, which sets the kV generator to the X-ray status (if all
interlocks are satisfied). On receipt of a TRIGGER pulse from the DCB, the kV Generator fires the
X-Ray tube to radiate the detector panel through the exposed object. This acquires an image. This
cycle continues while the foot switch is pressed continuously or the kV Start button on the
function key pad is pressed - so for each occurrence of the DCB TRIGGER, one X-ray pulse is
generated and one kV image is acquired.
The tube housing has a thermal cut-out for X-ray tube over temperature. The generator also
provides a 240 V AC power supply for the cooling fan on the X-ray tube, which is energized
continually when the generator is energized.
In the cabinet of the kV generator there are 2 Elekta-specific parts:
• Elekta Interface PCB
• Generator Adaptor assembly.
For a full description of the kV generator, refer to the documentation supplied by the
manufacturer.
4.6.1 Description of the Elekta Interface PCB in the SHF 435 DSI Series
generator (non-applicable for Harmony)
The kV generator is a stand-alone equipment that interfaces with other parts of the XVI system
and the host linear accelerator. A printed circuit board (PCB) installed in the kV generator cabinet
supplies the interface connections (see the XVI System diagrams PCB Interface EL, A-3592-02 F,
Sheet 01 of 01).
The Elekta Interface PCB has connections through the adapter assembly to the:
• DCB
• CITB
• XVI control cabinet (RS232/422 Serial communications)
• Function keypad (through an adapter assembly and the imaging breakout assembly)
• Interface cabinet or RIC.
The DCB sends the input signals through a 50 Ω BNC connector that is not terminated.

4.7 kV generator - Sedecal SHFR 400 DSI Series
The Sedecal SHFR 400 DSI Series generator is a 40 kW unit that can produce single and
continuous radiographic exposures of varying energy and dose.
When you press the kV START button on the function keypad kV enable signals the kV generator,

4.8.1 Description of the kV source arm
The kV source arm is installed on a pair of parallel arms that extend through the front of the
gantry drum. The kV source arms are not telescopic. They are held in position by a latch roller
assembly on the gantry drum. When the kV source arm is retracted, the kV source arms are fully
contained in the gantry drum.
The kV source arm can be manually extended or retracted as necessary. A push button on the
cover unlocks the kV source arm. When the kV source arm is unlocked, it can be manually
extended to its full length of travel to the imaging position. When locked in the imaging position,
the kV source arm enables part of the kV interlock chain.
When the kV source arm is unlocked, it can be manually pushed in the direction of the gantry
drum to its full length of travel, where it automatically locks.
A retractile cable guide is installed between the kV source arm and the linear accelerator. The
cable guide prevents damage to the cables of the kV source arm during the full range of
movement between the extended and retracted positions.

Figure 4.7 shows a representation of the shielding and collimation in the collimator housing.

Figure 4.7 Shielding and collimation in the collimator housing

The collimator housing is offset from the X-ray tube by 3.5° to achieve correct beam positioning at
the large FOV. Also, the collimator is angled at 3.5°.
The housing contains a primary collimator with an aperture that lets sufficient X-rays to pass
through the housing to encompass the different field of views, plus a margin. There is also a lead-
lined box structure with a secondary shielding plate to reduce leakage from the collimator
housing. The lead-lined box is 3.2 mm in thickness and has an X-ray absorption quality of 99.99%
TO (BS334 Type A).
The mounting assembly contains a thick plate that has an angled face to counteract the tilt angle
of the tube. The mounting plate also accommodates four stainless steel pillars that support the
collimator. The pillars effectively restrict any deflection of the collimator under its own weight.
An adjustable bracket assembly is fitted to the interface plate and bolted to the cable chain stand
off bracket. The adjustable brackets make sure that any load applied to the collimator when a
cassette is inserted is not transferred to the kV tube, causing the tube to twist. Instead, the load is
transferred to the tube mount casting.
The collimator also interfaces with the cover of the kV source arm. An aperture in the cover aligns
with the rails in the collimator housing allowing the collimator and filter cassettes to be inserted
and withdrawn.

The electrical connections at the collimator housing are not used in XVI R3.5.1 and R4.2.1
Collimator and filter encoding is a supported function of the electrical connections in XVI R4.5 and
subsequent releases.
Collimator and filter encoding is a supported function of the electrical connections in XVI R5.0 and
subsequent releases.

4.8.6 Electrical grounding of the collimator housing
The collimator housing has two aluminum rails in which the collimation and filtration cassettes
run. The two rails are grounded near their leading edges (edges where each cassette is inserted
from.) This ensures compliance to the IEC 60601-1 and UL 60601-1 standards.
4.8.7 Suspended mass of the collimator housing
The collimator housing is considered to be a suspended mass. The assembly is compliant to the
Suspended Mass clause of IEC 60601-1/UL 60601-1.
4.8.8 Secondary collimation and filtration of the X-rays
Secondary collimation is provided by cassettes placed in the collimator housing of the kV source.
There are three types of cassette:
• Collimator cassettes - which limit the X-ray beam differently by constraining it to a field
size when the kV detector panel is positioned at a certain FOV.
• Filter cassettes - which shape the collimated beam by applying pre-defined filtration,
for example an aluminum bow-tie filter.
• Calibration cassettes - which apply pre-defined beam attenuation and fiducial marks
for use during installation, service and maintenance of the XVI system.
Before a kV imaging session, a collimator cassette and a filter cassette are manually inserted into
the collimator housing by the user. These are the only beam shaping and filtration devices
encountered by the user during clinical use.

4.8.9 Construction of the cassettes
The handle of each cassette is marked with a different alphanumeric code that uniquely identifies
the cassette. For collimator cassettes, the code denotes the nominal field size obtained by the
collimator at a FOV: for example the code L20 indicates that the collimator cassette imposes a
nominal field size of 20 cm in the G–T direction at the isocenter when the kV detector panel is
positioned at the large FOV.

4.8.10 Collimator cassettes
Each collimator cassette contains a lead insert with an opening of a particular size at a particular
position. The opening corresponds to the beam shaping parameter that was prescribed at
treatment planning. The lead insert is 3.2 mm in thickness and has an X-ray absorption quality of
99.99% TO (BS334 Type A).
The collimator cassettes also contain sheets of clear extruded Polyethylene Terephthalate Glycol
(PETG) copolymer of 1 mm in thickness. The PETG sheets give protection to users if they touch
the lead material in the cassettes. The material is resistant to degradation in radiation and does
not change color. The Al equivalent of the acrylic sheets is given in IEC 60601-1-3.
XVI systems have cassettes with encoding PCBs. When they are inserted in the collimator
assembly, the cassettes type appears on the treatment room monitor (TRM).

The kV detector arm is installed at 180° to the kV source and is therefore at 90° to the iViewGT™
MV detector arm. With the gantry of the digital accelerator at 0°, the kV detector arm is
mechanically offset by 115 mm upwards from the isocenter to prevent interference with the
middle arm touchguard of the MV detector arm. The kV detector arm is also radially offset
towards the isocenter.
The base assembly of the kV detector arm contains and gives access to the DCB and the ACB. The
manual brake release buttons let the user move the kV detector panel longitudinally by hand.

The movements of the kV detector arm are controlled by the ACB. When a movement is
requested, the system logic is examined to confirm that the movement can be made before
electrical power is applied to the motor drives. The position of the kV detector arm is controlled
by potentiometers.
4.9.2 Open movement (non-applicable for Harmony)
The kV detector arm is moved from the closed position to the medium FOV position in a
combination of the pivot, longitudinal and lateral movements. The open movement is engaged
when these three conditions occur at the same time:
• The kV detector panel is in the closed position
• The <OPEN> button on the HHC is pressed
• The <ENABLE> button on the HHC is held pressed.
Before power is applied to the drive system, the microswitch logic and the potentiometer
voltages are examined. This makes sure that the system operates in valid movement conditions
only.
To pivot from the closed position to the open position, the microswitch conditions that follow
must agree:

• Locking pin microswitch (B-side of arm assembly) is not activated
• Locking pin microswitch (A-side of arm assembly) is not activated
• Detector-locked is in the locked status
• Touchguard is not activated.

The Open A (Pivot Open) locking pin microswitch assembly is also used as an interlock for the

The angular movement stops before the engagement of the locking pins, therefore the arm

reached when the solenoid withdraws and lets the longitudinal movement operate. It can be
heard when the solenoid withdraws.
The microswitches are continually monitored during the movement. The closed microswitch
senses if the arm is in the closed position. The Open A (Pivot Open) locking pin microswitch and
the Open B (Pivot Open) locking pin microswitch sense when the locking pins are engaged on
each side of the kV detector panel. A valid pivot demand overrides these microswitches. It is valid
for the arm to be neither fully open or fully closed during movement.

senses when the locking pins are engaged on the kV detector panel. A valid pivot demand
overrides these microswitches. It is valid for the arm to be neither fully open or fully closed
during movement.

When the pivot movement has completed, the locking solenoid is withdrawn. If the detector
locked microswitch senses that the solenoid is still engaged, the motorized longitudinal

When the <OPEN> and <ENABLE> buttons are pressed continuously, the kV detector panel is
moved to the medium FOV position by the longitudinal and the lateral movements. The
longitudinal and lateral potentiometers sense when the kV detector panel gets to the medium

If the middle arm retracted microswitch is not in the closed status when the pivot action has
completed, the arm logic is in an incorrect status. To correct this the middle arm will move back
until the microswitch is closed and then move the kV detector panel out. This could be confusing
to the user, but is usually not noticeable as it is only a small movement. This enables the correct
logic for the middle arm movements to be kept and the mechanical longitudinal scale to read
correctly.
4.9.3 Close movement (non-applicable for Harmony)
The Close movement moves the kV detector panel from any deployed position to the closed
position in a combination of lateral, longitudinal and pivot movements. The kV detector arm is
first moved to the lateral centered position, then retracted fully, and finally pivoted up into the
closed position.
When the <CLOSE> button on the XVI HHC is pressed while the <ENABLE> button is held pressed,
the lateral movement drives into the slow speed region of the movement and searches for the
Panel_Centered microswitch. When the microswitch is operated, the middle arm drives towards
the gantry until the Middle_Arm_Retracted microswitch is operated. The kV detector panel then
drives towards the gantry.
When the detector locking position microswitch is reached, the detector locking solenoid is
released and the movement continues to drive towards the gantry. As the detector locking slot
passes over the solenoid, the solenoid locking pin locates and actuates the Detector_Locked
microswitch.
If the detector was moved back to its maximum retracted position, so the detector locking slot is
nearer to the gantry than the locking solenoid, the detector is moved away from the gantry.
When the detector locking position microswitch is deactivated, the drive is reversed and the
detector is driven back towards the gantry. The solenoid is then released and locates into the
detector locking slot as the movement retracts, as described above.
The pivot system logic is examined for the following conditions:
• Lateral movement is centered (panel centered microswitch operated).
• The detector is locked by solenoid locking pin (Detector_Locked microswitch
operated).
The Open A (Pivot Open) locking pin microswitch PCB is an assembly with two microswitches. If
all conditions are in the correct status, the arm will start to pivot into the closed position. Before
the movement, the A-side and B-side locking pins are retracted and the two Open_A and Open_B
microswitches are deactivated. The power is prevented from being applied to the locking solenoid
as it is interlocked through the Open_A microswitch (preventing incorrect movement of the
cradle). However, if the panel centered or detector-locked microswitches go into an incorrect
status or if the movement is stopped, the interlock chain is broken and the buzzer sounds.
The Open A (Pivot Open) locking pin microswitch PCB is an assembly with two microswitches. If
all conditions are in the correct status, the arm will start to pivot into the closed position. Before
the movement, the A-side and B-side locking pins are retracted and the Open_A microswitch is
deactivated. The power is prevented from being applied to the locking solenoid as it is interlocked
through the Open_A microswitch (preventing incorrect movement of the cradle). However, if the

4.9.4 Center movement of the detector panel (Harmony only)
The center movement moves the detector panel from any position to the isocenter position.
Operation of the CENTER PANEL button and the ENABLE bars cause the longitudinal and the
lateral movements to move the MV detector panel to the isocenter position. The longitudinal and
lateral potentiometers are used to sense when the detector panel gets to the isocenter position.
4.9.5 Movement operation
The longitudinal movement comprises two separate actions:
• The kV detector panel moves with respect to the middle arm.
• The middle arm moves with respect to the shoulder casting.
These are referred to as the detector drive and middle arm drive respectively.
The two movements are controlled using a clutch and a brake to transfer the drive from one
motor.
When the kV detector panel is driven from the retracted position at the gantry end of the
longitudinal movement, initially the detector is moved with respect to the middle arm. The
detector cradle is moved to the detector imaging position (to minimize the backscatter into the
detector). The detector imaging position microswitch is actuated and movement is switched to
the middle arm drive. Longitudinal positioning is then achieved using the middle arm drive.
When the system is retracted, the middle arm is driven until the middle arm retracted
microswitch detects that the middle arm is fully retracted. Movement switches to the detector
cradle drive. The detector cradle can then be driven back until the detector retracted microswitch
is operated.
4.9.6 Illegal logic status
If the control logic of the longitudinal movement reaches a status where the control system does
not know the longitudinal position, the manual brake release buttons flash and the longitudinal
movement is disabled. This continues until the logic is corrected.
This condition occurs when:
• The detector cradle is NOT at the imaging position (so backscatter may be introduced
into the image), AND
• The middle arm is NOT fully retracted (so, for non-Harmony, the longitudinal scale is
incorrect; for Harmony, the covers are not aligned).
If the manual brake release is operated, the detector cradle can be manually moved to a correct
logic status, that is, a known position. By de-selecting the manual brake release, the arm can be
driven normally.
4.9.7 Isocenter pause
When approaching the imaging position, longitudinal movement is slowed down at
approximately 2 cm (depending on calibration) before the imaging position is reached. This is
independent of direction (not as visible as in the lateral movement), and permits a more accurate
XVI R5.0x Document ID: 1522605_06
Corrective Maintenance Manual
© Elekta Group 2021. All rights reserved. Page 65

Product Description
kV detector arm
pause. When the imaging position is reached, the movement is paused for approximately 1
second to allow the user to stop at the imaging position.
The longitudinal coarse potentiometer is used to determine the position at which to reduce the
movement speed. The potentiometer voltage is compared with a known voltage, which is set
during calibration.
Using a similar voltage comparator system, the fine potentiometer is used to detect when the
panel is at the imaging position. Movement is paused for approximately 1 second.
4.9.8 Longitudinal extended limit
When the longitudinal movement reaches the furthest position from the gantry, the longitudinal
coarse potentiometer is used to detect the limit using a voltage comparator. When the limit has
been reached, only movement away from the limit is possible, that is, towards the gantry.
4.9.9 Manual brake release
The longitudinal movement has a manual brake release which is operated by either of the two
buttons located on each side of the arm. When the manual brake release is operated, the
indicator lamps in the switches are switched off and the user can move the kV detector arm by
hand (in the longitudinal direction only).
When the manual brake release is selected, the movements of the middle arm and detector
assembly follow the same logic as when motorized movements are used.
If the detector is moved from the small, medium or large FOV towards the gantry, the middle arm
is initially allowed to move into the shoulder assembly. When the middle arm retracted
microswitch is operated, the brake on the middle arm drive is applied and the brake on the
detector drive is released, allowing the detector to be moved along the middle arm toward the
gantry.
The opposite logic applies if the detector is moved away from the gantry.
The manual brake release button is also used to indicate if the logic control is in an incorrect
status. The brake buttons will flash while the system is in an incorrect status and will return to
normal indication when the logic status is correct.
4.9.10 Lateral movement
The lateral movement is driven using a leadscrew system and does not have a manual brake
release.
4.9.11 Medium FOV pause
As with the longitudinal movement, the lateral movement decelerates when approaching the
medium FOV position. The speed is reduced approximately 1 cm before the medium FOV is
reached from either direction (depending on calibration). When the medium FOV is reached, the
movement is paused for approximately 1 second to allow the user to stop.
The lateral coarse potentiometer is used to determine the position at which the movement speed
is reduced. The potentiometer voltage is compared with a known voltage that is set during
calibration.
Using a similar voltage comparator system, the fine potentiometer is used to detect when the
detector is at the medium FOV position. Movement is paused for approximately 1 second.

4.9.12 Small and large FOV positions
The small and large FOV positions are the limits of the lateral drive. The lateral coarse
potentiometer is used to determine the limits of movement using a voltage comparator. When a
valid position is reached, the only possible movement is away from that position.
In the small FOV, to align the center of the panel with the center of the kV beam, the panel must
be driven towards the center axis of the kV source (115 mm), that is, the endpoint of the lateral
drive using the lateral movement button.
For large FOV, the panel must be driven away from the center axis of the kV source (75 mm), that
is, the endpoint of the lateral drive using the lateral movement button. If the user tries to leave
the kV panel in a position that is not one of the defined lateral imaging positions, an audible
warning is emitted.
Note: This is a different audible warning to that produced when the kV panel touchguard is activated.
Refer to the XVI Instructions for Use manual for more information.

4.9.14 kV detector panel
The kV detector panel consists of a detector and a fluorescent screen. The X-rays emitted by the
kV source excite the fluorescent screen and therefore produce light. The detector converts the
light into an electrical signal that is read by electronics that surround the panel. The electrical
signal is converted into image data and sent to the XVI control cabinet.
CAUTION 4.1
When the XVI imaging system is emitting kV radiation, make sure that only the central area of
the kV detector panel is irradiated. Do not irradiate the panel electronics. Long term or direct
exposure to radiation could degrade the kV detector panel.
4.9.15 Trigger signal
A synchronization connection is used to trigger the panel and to send a frame synchronization
signal from the panel to the detector control board (DCB) when a complete frame has been read.
For the AL detector panel, a frame grabber in the XVI control cabinet receives image data through
a data link.
Note: The AL detetor panel is non-applicable for Harmony.
For the AP detector panel, the XVI control cabinet receives image data with the Gigabit Ethernet
connection. The frame grabber is not a default for XVI control cabinet Mk6 or Mk6i.
4.9.16 LEDs on the kV detector panel
Two LEDs are visible on the kV detector panel. The LED furthest from the connector is on (green)
when the electrical power supply is present. The LED nearest to the connector flashes orange
when the panel is in external trigger mode, and flashes green when the panel is in free running
mode.
4.9.17 Amorphous silicon (a-Si) kV detector panel
The kV detector panel is a Perkin Elmer Amorphous Silicon (a-Si) detector. It has a resolution of
1024 x 1024 16-bit pixel images, with a panel size of 41 x 41 cm (approximately 26 x 26 cm at
isocenter).
Figure 4.20 shows how the detector is assembled.
A synchronization connection is used to trigger the kV detector panel and to send a frame sync
from the kV detector panel to the DCB when a complete frame was read.
Image data is read from the kV detector panel, through a data link into a framegrabber (except
the XVI control cabinet Mk6 and Mk6i) in the XVI control system.
The kV detector panel has two LEDs. The panel is energized when the green LED is on. It is the
LED, that is farthest from the connector. The LED nearest the connectors flashes orange when it is
triggered by the external trigger from the DCB, and flashes green when the kV detector panel is in
free running mode.

4.9.18 Mechanical position indicators on the kV detector panel (non-
applicable for Harmony)
The position of the kV detector panel is indicated by a longitudinal scale and three lateral position
marks (L, M and S). The longitudinal scale is fixed to the arm and is graduated in increments of 0.5
cm and scaled to the isocenter. The three lateral position marks appear on both sides of the arm.
One set is visible through an aperture in the touchguard cover (as shown in Figure 4.21), the
other set are on the cradle assembly. The lateral position markers indicate whether the panel is
positioned for the Large, Medium or Small field of view.
The mechanical indicators are for general guidance only and are not suitable for accurate
positioning of the kV detector panel during clinical and service use.

4.9.19 Fields of view
To acquire images, the kV detector panel must be at one of three positions. This position is called
the field of view (FOV). These positions are the:
• Medium FOV
• Small FOV
• Large FOV.
The bottom of the kV detector panel is labelled with the characters S, M and L. When the panel is
at the related position, the applicable character aligns with a datum mark on the middle arm
touchguard. A full description of the field of view positions is given in sections
• Medium field of view position
• Small field of view position
• Large field of view position.

4.9.20 Modes of image acquisition
The table gives a list and description of the different modes of kV image acquisition for the XVI
system.
Acquisition Description
PlanarView™ In this mode, the linear accelerator gantry is static and XVI acquires one
static planar image. A number of frames are acquired and averaged to
display one image.
MotionView™ In this mode, XVI acquires a sequence of planar images for a period of time,
while the gantry of the linear accelerator moves or is static. The image
sequence can therefore show the movement of anatomical structures that
are in the field of view.
VolumeView™ This mode includes Symmetry™, and XVI acquires images while the gantry of
the linear accelerator rotates. Elekta recommends that the minimum gantry
rotation you use for a small field of view (FOV) is 200°. The maximum gantry
rotation is 360°.
An acquisition with a 360° rotation of the gantry acquires approximately 650
images. XVI saves each projection image and the gantry angle at acquisition.
XVI uses this data to reconstruct a 3D image of the anatomical volume. You
can then register this 3D volume, in XVI, against an imported CT reference
image.
Symmetry Symmetry VolumeView™ is the acquisition of a 4D VolumeView™ image.
VolumeView™ During 4D VolumeView™, XVI acquires images and reconstructs them in
relation to a period of time. This lets you see the different phases of the
breathing cycle for a patient.
To do a Symmetry VolumeView™ acquisition you must decrease the gantry
speed. XVI can then acquire sufficient projection data to collect multiple
phase data at each gantry angle. When XVI collects a sufficient number of
projection images, it then puts these images into a specified number of
phases (specified in the Symmetry preset) for XVI to reconstruct into 3D
images. XVI puts the projection images in sequence in relation to the largest
object that has the same movement as respiratory cycle movement in the
image. This sequence of 3D images makes up the Symmetry VolumeView™
image.
It is necessary to have a minimum gantry rotation of 200° for a Symmetry
VolumeView™ acquisition. A Symmetry VolumeView™ acquisition is
approximately 1400 images. The number of phases is specified in the
Symmetry preset as 10 (see the XVI Instructions for Use manual).
The more phases you have for your Symmetry VolumeView™ image, the
more information you have during the breathing cycle. But, a large number
of phases increases the time of the acquisition, and can decrease the image
quality.

imaging images during an MV treatment field delivery. This can allow the user to:

Do not go into the treatment room when the radiation warning lamp is on. Only the patient is

4.10 Detector control board (DCB)
For non-Harmony, the DCB is located behind the B-side cover of the base assembly of the kV
detector arm. For Harmony, the DCB is located in the B-side of chassis casting under base cover.
The functions of the DCB are to:
• Generate the frame-trigger pulses required for reading out images from the kV
detector
• Generate a trigger signal to enable the pulsing of the kV radiation (when required)
• Synchronize the kV image readout and the generation of kV radiation
• Time stamp the gantry readout
• Time stamp the kV expose trigger.
4.10.1 Row-trigger signal generation
The DCB receives XVI control CAN messages from the XVI control cabinet. A control message
contains an ENABLE FRAME READOUT bit that specifies whether the kV detector should be read.
If the ENABLE FRAME READOUT bit is asserted (logic 1), the DCB generates the frame trigger
signals necessary to read image frames from the kV detector. At the end of each complete frame,
a frame counter (8-bit) is incremented and then delayed for 40 ms before commencing to read
the next frame.
If the ENABLE FRAME READOUT bit in a received control message is not asserted (logic 0), the
DCB completes the readout of the current frame and then inhibits the triggering of any further
images until receipt of the next control message in which the ENABLE FRAME READOUT bit is
asserted. The frame counter is reset when frame readout is enabled.
XVI R5.0x Document ID: 1522605_06
Corrective Maintenance Manual
© Elekta Group 2021. All rights reserved. Page 79

Product Description
Arm control board (ACB)
4.10.2 Trigger signal generation
The XVI control messages also contain a MAXIMUM PULSE COUNT parameter that is used to
enable or disable the triggering of the kV generator during the periods between frame reads.
When the DCB receives the message, it records the embedded MAXIMUM PULSE COUNT in an
internal pulse counter. If the pulse count is greater than zero, the DCB generates a kV trigger pulse
at the start of the 40 ms period between each kV detector frame readout, and then decrements
the count. When the count reaches zero, no further kV pulses are enabled.
The DCB has an on-board millisecond timer that is used to time stamp all CAN messages that are
generated.
The DCB pulses the kV generator by sending a 1 ms trigger pulse over a co-axial link. The falling
edge of this pulse triggers the kV generator.
Even when kV radiation is not required, the DCB maintains an 40 ms delay between the readout
of each kV imaging frame. This makes sure that the frame time for kV image acquisition remains
constant, independent of kV exposure parameters.
4.11 Arm control board (ACB)
For non-Harmony, the ACB is behind the A-side cover of the arm base assembly. For Harmony, the
ACB is located in the A-side of chassis casting under base cover. The ACB controls the movements
system of the kV detector arm. It contains all input decoding, interlocks and power outputs.
The ACB has LEDs to help with the troubleshooting on the arm movement system. The LEDs are
divided into columns for each movement. They have the two modes of operation that follow:
• NORMAL
• EXTRA.
The modes of operation can be selected with a jumper that is moved to a different position.
4.11.1 Physical characteristics of the ACB
The ACB resides within the base assembly of the kV detector arm, behind the cover on the A side
of the arm assembly. The board is installed on insulating standoffs and an aluminum bar on the
left edge of the board. The aluminum bar also acts as a heat sink between the linear and PWM
based power devices to the shoulder casting. This provides adequate thermal mass, so forced air
cooling of the power devices on the ACB is not required.
The outline of the PCB and its fixing holes are defined by the shoulder casting and the heat sink
bar. Insulation is provided behind the ACB to minimize the occurrence of shorting leads on the
metallic parts behind the board.
For non-Harmony, the cover on the A side of the arm assembly provides physical and regulatory
protection. For Harmony, the base cover provides physical and regulatory protection. You can see
the ACB diagnostic LEDs through the cutout in the covers.
4.11.2 Machine interfaces
All the user interfaces necessary to operate the arm movements are filtered and authorized by
the ACB. These include the movement demand buttons of the XVI HHC, the HHC enable and the
manual brake release buttons, and illumination drive.
The ACB also contains an audible buzzer similar to devices used elsewhere on the host digital
accelerator, but pulsed for ease of identification.

4.11.4 Supplies and interlocks
Arm and host touchguard chains
A +24 V DC fused supply is connected in series to the detector touchguard chain and the middle
arm touchguard chain. The chain returns signal TGs_Healthy. The touchguard switches are
configured such that +24 V on TP13 signifies a healthy touchguard (that is, no collision indicated).
This makes sure that a fault such as a broken lead within the chains fails safe.
The TGs_Healthy signal closes the contacts of two relays (RL3 and RL1). The coils of these relays
are connected in parallel and the contacts are connected in series into the touchguard chain of
the host digital accelerator.
XVI R5.0x Document ID: 1522605_06
Corrective Maintenance Manual
© Elekta Group 2021. All rights reserved. Page 81

Product Description
Arm control board (ACB)
Field effect transistor TR6 makes sure that the relays are closed only if firmware output
Touchguard_Interlock is valid (logic 1). By using TR6, the firmware of the ACB can break the
touchguard chain of the digital accelerator - irrespective of the status of the detector or middle
arm touchguards.
For example if the kV detector arm is left between the open and closed positions, the touchguard
chain of the host digital accelerator is broken by TR6. This inhibits gantry rotation (unless the
touchguard override of the digital accelerator is used). The Touchguard_Interlock signal derives
from the interlocks_and_local_enable block within the firmware.
Note: The example above is for non-Harmony only.
Supplies monitor
IC9 monitors the +5 V, +15 V, + 24 V, -15 V and -24 V supply rails. If any supply falls below a
threshold level, then the Supplies_OK output goes low (logic 0). This output is monitored by the
firmware and forms part of the interlocks_and_local_enable block within the firmware.
+10 V reference generation
A positive 10 V reference for positional feedback in the coarse and fine potentiometer
transducers is derived from the +15 V linearly regulated supply rail of the ACB. Current limiting is
provided by a current feedback circuit (R134, etc.). A test point and a discrete LED are provided
for measurement and visual indication.
Switched ±24 V generation
Depending on the status of the host digital accelerator, a nominal +26 V signal (motor drive
output) from the digital accelerator is used to drive relay RL2 on the ACB. The common 0 V
connection between the ACB and the digital accelerator is at the HHC, so RL2 makes sure that
signal Host_Movements_PSU_0V is isolated from the 0 V rail of the ACB. This arrangement
minimizes 0V/GND loops within the system.
The closure of relay RL2 closes two power relays (RL4 and RL5). The latter generate the switched
±24 V DC supplies that drive the pivot, longitudinal and lateral motors of the kV detector arm. The
closure of RL4 and RL5 depends upon the status of FETs TR9 and TR10. Transistor TR9 and its
associated driver circuitry make sure that RL4 and RL5 are not powered unless signal Config_Done
is valid. Therefore, until the field programmable gate array (FPGA) reports successful
configuration, the ±24 V DC supplies are not present. The FPGA is IC13 on the ACB.
Transistor TR10 is a similar interlock to TR6 in the touchguard circuitry, allowing the firmware to
remove the power drive to RL4 and RL5. Signal PWR_Relay_Interlock is generated within the
interlocks_and_local_enable block of the firmware. This enables the ACB to respond instantly to a
touchguard fault.
The TGs_Healthy signal is monitored within the firmware and, if the local touchguard chain is
broken, the supply to RL4 and RL5 is removed by means of TR10 for a nominal period of two
seconds. By removing the Host_Movements_PSU_+26V signal, this arrangement ensures a faster
response, of minimum duration, than that provided by the digital accelerator.
LED indication of the switched and unswitched ±24 V DC supplies is provided:
• LED indication (and a test point) is provided for the Config_Done signal.
• Indication of the logical status of the PWR_Relay_Interlock is provided by the bargraph
diagnostic LEDs.
Test points are also provided.
Local power rail generation
A positive 15 V supply is linearly generated from the +24 V dc supply to the ACB. The +5 V rail is
also linearly generated from +24 V. A power resistor (R1) is used to minimize the power dissipated
within the +5 V regulator. The 3V3 supply is generated from the +5 V supply and has current limit
protection and thermal shutdown.

Arm control board (ACB)
A linearly regulated -15 V supply rail is generated from the -24 V DC supply to the ACB.
TransZorb® protection is provided on the ±24 V supply rails to minimize the harmful effect of high
voltage transients on the connected dc supply rails.
Buzzer
Buzzer BZ1 provides an audible indication that the host touchguard chain has been broken by the
kV detector arm. Supply configuration is provided (the default is +15 V). The buzzer is driven by
TR8 which, in turn, is driven by the firmware for flexibility of control. The buzzer is pulsed to
differentiate its tone from other sources of indication. Control signal Buzzer from the FPGA
derives from the interlocks_and_local_enable block within the firmware.
Spare devices
The spare or unused device pins (IC12 pins 5,6 and 7) are connected to discrete resistors to
enable in-circuit testing of the operational amplifier.
4.11.5 Input conditioning
Digital inputs
All inputs are protected with a series 1KW 1W resistor, the output of which is connected through
a Schottky diode to the relevant power rail and GND. This protects the inputs from inadvertent
connection to the maximum on-board supply rail. The series resistor also provides RC filtering in
conjunction with a ceramic capacitor to GND.
With the exception of the filtered, buffered, movement potentiometer inputs, all inputs (after
filtering) are directly connected to the FPGA.
Some potential division is applied to the +24 V TGs_Healthy signal and the
Host_Movements_PSU_On signal.
Movement potentiometer inputs
The inputs from the analog movement potentiometers are buffered by an operational amplifier
after filtering to ensure a known output impedance to the next stage.
4.11.6 Altera
Oscillators
The oscillator is a single chip at an operating frequency of 10 MHz. The output enable pin (pin 1)
is pulled high by a discrete resistor to disable the output during production testing. The clock
output is connected directly to a dedicated input (DI1) on the FPGA. Within the FPGA, this signal
is clocked down for specific functions.
Watchdog
The watchdog device monitors the 3V3 supply rail and the presence of an indication of a clock on
the WDI input (pin 4). This signal is a direct output from the FPGA. Within the FPGA the Watchdog
output is connected to the clock input.
The watchdog acknowledges a fault by pulling the nReset input on pin 1 low (logic 0). This is
connected directly to the FPGA (the second dedicated input) - DI2 (pin 28). This is
“Watchdog_nReset” within the firmware and is used within the interlocks_and_local_enable
block.
EPROM
An EPC1 type EPROM (single shot programmable) is used.
FPGA
A EPF6024AQC208-3 device is used within this assembly. A summary of the device's technical
features utilized within this design may be seen as follows.

4.11.7 Switched supplies monitors
+24 V switched supply monitor
A comparator is used to verify that the +24V switched supply is present. Hysteresis is provided by
R257. The output from the circuit, "+24VSW_Present", is monitored by the firmware. It provides
the firmware with the status of the power relay RL4. Therefore if the relay is 'stuck closed' it may
be determined.
-24 V switched supply monitor
This circuit is similar to that used to monitor the +24V switched supply (described in the previous
section). The input is first inverted (unity gain inverter) before comparison. Diode D81 protects
the comparator input from -ve potentials. The output, "-24VSW_Present", is monitored by the
firmware.
+24 V switched brake supply monitor
This circuit is identical in configuration to that used to monitor the +24V switched supply. The
output, "+24VSwBrk_Present", is monitored by the firmware.
+24 V switched clutch supply monitor
This circuit is identical in configuration to that used to monitor the +24V switched supply. The
output, "+24VswClu_Present", is monitored by the firmware.
4.11.8 Longitudinal limits and imaging position
Longitudinal extension limit
The filtered and buffered longitudinal coarse potentiometer input is compared with an adjustable
threshold using a LM339 type comparator. Positive feedback is provided to ensure hysteresis. The
open collector output is pulled up to +5V and the output monitored by the firmware. The
'nLong_Coarse_Extend_Limit' output informs the firmware that the calibrated end of the
longitudinal travel in the T-direction (extension, that is, towards the Target).
Longitudinal isocenter
The imaging position of the kV detector arm is defined by a series of voltage thresholds. These
thresholds generate virtual windows of distance on the travel. Figure 4.33 shows the signal
names that are used to define the virtual windows. Isocenter is reached when the logic
acknowledges that the inputs show the movement to be within the coarse window and the fine
window.

Figure 4.33 Longitudinal isocenter virtual windows
The comparator circuits generate the logical inputs to the FPGA. Calibration is conducted by
means of adjusting the reference voltage. The adjustment is made by means of the 10 turn
vertical potentiometers on the ACB. Footprints have been provided to allow removal of the
potentiometer and 'fixing' of the threshold. Hysteresis is provided within the comparator stages.
The threshold level comparator input is pulled to either the +10V reference supply rail or GND to
ensure that failure of the reference deriving potentiometer wiper is controlled (that is, the input
is not left floating).
The movement potentiometer inputs increases linearly as the movement retracts (as shown by
the sloped line in the figure above. This follows the convention used within the NPSS movements
scheme and is hoped to minimize confusion.
Within the 'coarse window' the maximum speed is limited. Therefore approaching it at maximum
speed, the speed (voltage applied to the motor) is reduced. Upon reaching the 'fine window' the
movement stops for a nominal period of one second. After this time period the applied voltage to
the motor (proportional to speed) is ramped up to a reduced maximum value until the movement
exits the 'coarse window' when the speed is ramped up to a maximum speed again.
The speed is reduced on approaching the 'fine window' to optimise the positional accuracy by
reducing the stopping distance.
Note: For non-Harmony, when using the 'auto close' function (Pivot Close HHC Button) the coarse and
fine window inputs are ignored to optimize the closure time.
Spare devices
The spare or unused device pins (IC12 pins 1,6 & 7) are connected to discrete resistors to enable
in-circuit testing of the comparator.

Large and small FOVs

Product Description
Arm control board (ACB)
The principles and circuitry used for lateral movement limits are similar to those in the
longitudinal movements. Whereas only the fully extended position of the longitudinal drive is
interpreted using the coarse potentiometer, both motion drive limits are derived on the lateral
drive.
Full extension on the A side is signaled by the Lat_Coarse_Limit_A signal at logic 0: full extension
on the B side is signaled by the Lat_Coarse_Limit_B signal at logic 0. As implied by the
corresponding signal names, the coarse movement potentiometer is used to derive the limit
positions.
These output signals are direct FPGA inputs. Therefore drive is logically disabled upon reaching
the limit, and only the drive movement away from the limit is logically allowed. Hysteresis is
provided by positive feedback on the comparators, and calibration of the end limits is provided by
the on-board multi-turn potentiometers. The A limit is of lower potential than the B limit.
Lateral isocenter
The principle for deriving the isocenter on the lateral movement is the same as that employed for
the longitudinal drive. Figure 4.34 shows the coarse and fine windows. Control of the motor
when within these windows is identical to that described for the longitudinal drive. Note: the
calibrated isocentric position does not verify that the lateral is centered within the Chassis
Casting. This 'centered' location is identified by the 'Det_Centered' microswitch signal (active
low). This enables the calibrated isocenter and the lateral's 'center' position to be different,
therefore the 'center' position is not dependent upon the correct calibration or correct
functionality of the lateral potentiometers. It also allows the 'center' position to the defined
during manufacture of the arm and negates 'center' setup on site by the Installation Engineer. The
movement potentiometer outputs increase as the axis moves towards the 'B' side as shown in
Figure 4.34.
