4.11.11 Locking solenoid (non-applicable for Harmony)
Solenoid driver
The solenoid driver source is the Burr-Brown DRV102T device. This is a controllable PWM (pulse
width modulation) device. This allows the solenoid to be driven at 100% duty cycle for a fixed
time period, and then at a lower duty cycle. This minimizes the power delivered to the solenoid
and therefore increases the system efficiency and minimizes the heat dissipated in the solenoid.
Note: the solenoid may be 'held' with a lower voltage once the pin has been withdrawn due to
the magnetic characteristics of such devices. The device is controlled by the FPGA. When the
FPGA output "Solenoid_Driver_input" is a logical one, the solenoid driver is 'on'. C20 allows
adjustment of the initial time period the device provides 100% duty cycle output. R9 adjusts the
output (for example, 50%) for the remaining period for which the output is applied (when the
"IN" input is logic one). The active low 'Flag' output indicates when the output is open circuit.
Note: this is seen to oscillate for such an inductive type load. The PWM output is filtered using L1
and C49.
For more information on this device refer to the manufacturer's datasheet.
Interlock
The filtered PWM output is switched using TR3, which is controlled by the FPGA. When the FPGA
output "ARM_Open" in a logic one, the FET TR3 in turned 'on'.
Logical conditions (firmware)
The logical conditions for the FPGA outputs may be seen in the two following sections.
Note: The solenoid is released by a spring when the device is not energized.
FPGA Output "Solenoid_Driver_Input"
XVI R5.0x Document ID: 1522605_06
Corrective Maintenance Manual
© Elekta Group 2021. All rights reserved. Page 87

Product Description
Arm control board (ACB)
This output may be considered as the logical output of a five input AND gate. The inputs to this
AND gate are described as follows. This gate may be seen in the "acb.gdf" file using the MAX+plus
II Software.
• nRelease_Solenoid
an active low signal from the "auto_move_module". This module requests the release
of the solenoid during 'auto closure' of the arm. The solenoid is pre-released during
longitudinal retraction, before emulating a demand to close the arm.
• Pivot_Open
an active high signal from the "interlocks_and_local_enable" module.
• nReset
an active low signal, the output of a two input AND gate. This two inputs are
"Supplies_OK" and "PL11" which lets the FPGA to be 'reset' by with a jumper on the
ACB.
• Watchdog_nReset
an active low 'reset' signal from the on-board watchdog device.
• nValid_Dir_Close
an active low signal, the inversion of the "Valid_Dir_Close" signal. This signal is the
logical AND condition of four signals. Most notably are the two signals originating from
the "pivot_dem_dir_generation" module - "Valid_Demand" and "Direction". Therefore,
for "Valid_Dir_Close" to be logical one, a valid demand must be granted for closing.
FPGA Output "Arm_Open"
This signal is derived within the "interlocks_and_local_enable" block.
The inputs to the logic above are the active low signals from the corresponding microswitches
within the arm as follows:
• nPivot_Open_A
an active low signal, logic zero when the locking pin on the 'A' side of the arm is sensed
to be in it's 'locked position'.
• nPivot_Open_B
an active low signal, logic zero when the locking pin on the 'B' side of the arm is sensed
to be in it's 'locked position'.
• nPivot_Closed
an active low signal, logic zero when the cam on the 'A' side of the arm activates the
microswitch.
It may be seen that all three microswitch inputs must be in the correct position for the
"Pivot_Open" signal to be logic one. A dormant fault of one of these microswitches is
detected within the "interlocks_and_local_enable" module.
If the output of the three input OR gate is a logical zero then the
"Touchguard_Interlock" is logical zero, therefore the host touchguard chain is not
made (refer to Supplies and interlocks)
All three microswitch inputs used to derive "Pivot_Open" are used to derive the
"Pivot_Closed" signal. During pivoting of the arm, these three microswitches will not
meet the logical conditions for 'Pivot_Open' or 'Pivot_Closed', therefore
"Pivot_Moving" is used to ensure that the host touchguard chain is not broken (but
may be broken for other reasons such as a touchguard fault) disabling movement.
Therefore in both the 'open' and 'closed' positions a dormant short-circuit fault of the
'unused' microswitch should be detected (that is, closed microswitch when the arm is
open).

4.11.12 Longitudinal motor drive
Demand control
The demand to the motor drive stage is an 8 bit digital word, an output from the FPGA. This byte
is the input to the DAC08, an eight bit DAC. The output of the convertor is a current output. The
device is used in a bipolar output configuration. When the MSB input is logical one and all other
inputs are logical zero, the output IOUT should be zero current. This has the effect of zero voltage
drive to the motor. When all the inputs are logical one, then the maximum current is sinked (-ve
current) by pin IOUT resulting in a positive output from the power operational amplifier. When all
the inputs are logical zero, then the maximum current is sourced (+ve current) by pin IOUT
resulting in a negative output from the power operational amplifier. The current output and
feedback resistor on the power operational amplifier are such that for inputs 11111111 and
00000000 the power operational amplifier output is pushed into saturation. This limits the power
dissipated for these two demand extremes and allows speed control (voltage output) to be
defined within the firmware.
Logical enable
The power operational amplifier has an enable pin (E/S). When the device is disabled, the output
may be compared with the 'tri-status' condition common in digital devices (refer to the Burr-
Brown datasheet for further information). The E/S pin is pulled low to disable. This pin is driven
low in it's 'default' condition by the n-p-n transistor TR28. The FPGA has control over the base
drive of this transistor and allows the transistor to be turned 'off', therefore the E/S pin is
released 'high' and the output is enabled.
Power operational amplifier
A number of the OPA549 device features have already been explained in this section. Other
features used are:
As well as acting as an enable pin, the E/S pin indicates the thermal shutdown status of the
device. The device pulls this pin low, indicating that the device is in thermal shutdown. This pin is
connected to the FPGA and indication of the status of this pin is provided by one of the red
bargraph diagnostic LEDs.
The ILIM pin this allows the current limit to be defined by a resistor to GND. A 10 nF parallel
capacitor minimizes the effect of noise. The current limit for the longitudinal axis is approximately
3 A.
4.11.13 Output monitoring
Hardware monitoring
The output voltage of the power operational amplifier is monitored by a series of comparator
circuits. Two active high outputs are generated. The signal Long_+VE_O/P indicates that the
motor drive output is positive, while the signal Long_-VE_O/P is negative. The threshold voltage is
nominally 1 volt, (+1VR). The output is potentially divided by R337, R334 combination. The BAS19
Schottky diode is used to minimize the voltage drop before monitoring by the LM339
comparators. The sensitivity threshold is therefore just over ±4 V on the motor drive output.
Firmware monitoring
The hardware outputs are monitored within firmware. This monitor has knowledge of the
expectation of the output (primarily a factor of the logical demand) and the hardware monitoring
feedback. If the feedback indicates an output with no demand or the monitor indicates no
feedback yet there is a valid demand an 'error' has occurred. During transient conditions (for
example, ramp up of the output) this 'error' is not valid. A counter is used to verify the validity of
the 'error'. This counter is allowed to count up and down, this repeated transient conditions do
not accumulate in the incorrect verification of such errors. Monitoring of the two conditions
provides dormant fault protection of the monitoring hardware.

4.11.16 Manual brake release LED drive
The illumination of the manual brake release buttons is controlled by the FPGA. The output is
switched +24 V power, using a p-n-p type transistor (TR26). This device is controlled by the n-p-n
type transistor TR27.
Flashing the MBR button is provided to indicate to the user that the longitudinal movement is out
of sequence, that is, with the kV detector panel not in the imaging position (at the T end of the
middle arm) the middle arm must be retracted.
During auto opening, the middle arm is retracted automatically if it is not already retracted (as
indicated by the Middle_Arm_Retracted microswitch) before the extension of the kV detector
panel.

4.12 Electrical power supplies
The XVI system requires mains electrical power for the kV detector arm and for the XVI control
cabinet and its peripherals.
4.12.1 kV generator power supply
The kV generator can operate from one of two mains electrical power supplies:
• 400 V ac (±10%), 3-phase, 50/60 Hz
• 480 V ac (±10%), 3-phase, 50/60 Hz (USA only).
4.12.2 XVI power supply units
Electrical power for the kV detector panel and arm movements is derived from the host digital
accelerator. The power is provided by two power supply units (PSUs):
Detector PSU Arm movement PSU.
The PSUs are installed in AREA 44 at the rear of the gantry arm of the digital accelerator.
The PSUs are configured to provide output power rails of +24 V and -24 V.

Description of the function keypad
Systems can also have the XVI/iViewGT™ PSU chassis installed. Systems with the combined XVI/
iViewGT™ PSU chassis have LED indicators on the +24V and the -24V PSU to show that the output
supply is present.
4.12.3 XIM power supply
The XIM gets its power from the iViewGT™ power supply and uses the XIM power breakout cable.
In systems with the combined XVI/iViewGT™ PSU assembly, the XIM is energized from the XVI/
iViewGT™ PSU assembly.
4.12.4 XVI control cabinet power supplies
The XVI control cabinet, monitor and line driver are supplied with electrical power by the
distribution unit of the TCS. Any other peripherals (for example a local printer) must be connected
to local mains electrical sockets.
4.12.5 Earthing (grounding)
All parts must be effectively earthed (that is, at least to IEC regulations).
The XVI control cabinet, the monitor and the printer must be earthed (grounded) to the local
supply through the mains cables.
The arm control PSU, the arm assembly, the kV source and the kV detector panel are earthed to
the host digital accelerator.
Note: Elekta cannot accept responsibility for damage to equipment as a result of fluctuating mains
supplies or inadequate earthing (grounding).
4.13 Description of the function keypad
Note: Each configuration of the linear accelerator has a different FKP. Not all FKPs have kV buttons. On
some FKPs, the colors of the buttons can be different, but their function stays the same.

4.14 XVI controls
The XVI controls consist of the TRM, the HHC, the FKP and the GUI, mouse and keyboard of the
XVI control cabinet. The other manual features of the XVI system are the handle and lock push
button of the kV source arm, and the collimation, filtration and calibration cassettes.
4.14.1 Treatment room monitor (TRM)
Information from the XVI program can be displayed on one TRM of the host digital accelerator.
For non-Harmony, the display can be switched from the TRM to the XVI monitor with the top left
button on the XVI HHC or the Linac HHC. For Harmony, the display can be switched from the TRM
to the XVI monitor with the IVIEW button on the HHC.
4.14.2 Handheld controller (HHC)
For non-Harmony, the XVI HHC in the treatment room can be located on the A-side or the B-side
of the host digital accelerator, as required by the hospital. For Harmony, the HHC with XVI control
buttons is on the T-end of the treatement table.
The HHC has buttons that are used to control the movement of the kV detector arm and panel.
The buttons are operative only when the user also presses and holds one or the two of the enable
bars on each side of the HHC. See Figure 4.38.

The non-Harmony XVI system has three different touchguards. When a touchguard is touched,

Image correction
The Harmony XVI system has a detector panel touchguard. If the touchguard is hit, it activates,
and stops all movement of the linear accelerator, detector arm, and treatment table. A
Touchguard inhibit appears on the treatment control system.

4.15 Image correction
4.15.1 Offset image corrections (black level)
While the XVI program is running, the DCB continually triggers the kV detector, with a pause of
182 ms between frames, and the software continually acquires images - even when the radiation
is off.
When the software detects that the radiation has been off for more than a specified number of
frames (AutoOffsetMinBeamOffFrames) and enough frames have been captured
(AutoOffsetMinFrames) to generate an offset image, a new offset image is created and
stored in memory. The offset is then applied to any subsequently acquired images.
The XVI program continually monitors the CAN messages from the DCB. A kV DCB inhibit appears,
if there is no communication to the kV DCB.
4.15.2 Gain correction
To optimize the image quality, a gain calibration is required at each energy. This corrects for the
differences in gain response of each of the pixels at different energies, and also calibrates the
image dose.
You can choose between Single Level Gain (SLG) and Multi Level Gain(MLG). You can do gain
calibration from the Service menu (Service > kV Service Functions > SLG and Filter calibration).
The number of energies required to be calibrated is set by the presets. When this is set, the
calibration procedure automatically takes the user through the gain calibration.
4.15.3 Filter correction
To improve the kV scatter correction and reconstruction uniformity, a filter calibration is
necessary at each energy. This corrects for the filter profile which is introduced in the image when
you use the filter. XVI calculates a normalization factor to correct for exposure.
4.15.4 Bad pixel map
A bad pixel map is used to identify image pixels that do not respond correctly and cannot be
corrected by using gain or offset. To overcome this condition, the value of the bad pixel is set to
the average value of the neighboring pixels.
For a new kV detector panel, a bad pixel map is supplied on an accompanying compact disc
(PerkinElmer Opto-electronics). This image is copied into the XVI directory during the installation
process. If the bad pixel map changes during the life of the kV detector panel, it can be updated
by using the XVI program.

Product Description
Log files
The data acquired from a bad pixel map is presented by the XVI program as a dialog at the XVI
control cabinet. Figure 4.41 is an example of a Bad Pixel Statistics Results dialog box.
NOT TO SCALE 002823 ©2011 Elekta Limited
Figure 4.41 Example of Bad Pixel Statistics Results dialog
In the Details panel of Figure 4.41 it can be seen that the statistical data is presented in columns.
The Statistic column lists each separate statistic that was measured during bad pixel map
acquisition.
The Acq columns lists the values obtained for each statistic during successive pixel map
acquisitions.
Below the Details panel, the Summary field shows the total number of bad pixels found, and the
percentage of the kV detector panel area that is affected by bad pixels.
A bad pixel algorithm in the XVI program calculates the statistics of the bad pixel map. The
algorithm uses ±3 standard deviation to create a range of satisfactory values that may include
negative numbers at the bottom of the range. The presence of negative numbers is normal.
4.15.5 Software triggering and image capture
When the user requests acquisition of an image, the software loads the appropriate calibration
images from memory.
The raw image is then corrected using offset and gain images and the bad pixel map. In the kV
detector panel (AL), the image is then rotated through 90° and displayed. In the kV detector panel
(AP), the image is not rotated.
Note: The AL detector panel is not applicable for Harmony.
The XVI program continually monitors the CAN messages from the DCB, as it does during the
automatic offset correction. If a message fails to appear for 10 seconds, the following error
message is displayed: Communication lost with the DCB
If the user acknowledges this message and a message is not received from the DCB within
another 10 seconds, the error message will be displayed again.
4.16 Log files
Log files are generated for general XVI and DICOM events. By default the log files are written to
c:\xvi\logs\. The log files are used to find faults.
New Log files can also be appended to the log files, or cleared each time XVI is started.

External tools
If LogAppend is set to 1 in the sri.ini file, the log will be appended. If it is set to 0, the log
file will erased and a new log file will be generated each time XVI is started.
4.17 External tools
Programs external to the XVI program can be launched by the service user by selecting the
Service menu, followed by External Tools.
The programs to be launched and text to be displayed in the menu is configured in the sri.ini
file under [ExternalTools].
Labelx = The text to be displayed in the External Tools menu.
Programx = The path to the corresponding program.
Note: Elekta is not responsible for any external programs launched from External Tools in the XVI
program, nor the effects thereof, except for the default programs.
4.18 DCB CAN test
A facility is supplied to read the data from the DCB over the CAN link, this is run by the DCB
Utility.
4.19 Extended Imaging Module (XIM)
The Extended Imaging Module (XIM) is installed in AREA 13 of the gantry. The XIM changes the
analogue voltages from the kV detector arm into digital CAN messages for interpretation by the
XVI control cabinet.
Refer also to XVI system diagrams.
Note: The XIM communicates with the XVI control cabinet through the MV CAN bus, which is extended
from the iViewGT™ control cabinet to the XVI control cabinet.
The XIM takes various analogue and digital inputs from MV (Mega Volt) Arm Control Board, kV
(Kilo Volt) Arm Control Board and from collimator cassettes and filter cassettes. Five CAN
daughter boards change all these analogue inputs into useful CAN information.
In addition, the Daughter boards provide Digital IO to allow the system to generate key presses on
the hand held controllers (HHCs) from software on both the kV Arm and MV Arm. The Digital IO
also provides the facility to control the function key pad gantry ASU signal.
Finally the Digital IO provides a number of digital inputs. These are the kV gating signal from the
IBA and from the function key pad, a feedback signal from the gantry ASU signal, and finally a
signal from the IBA that indicates if there is a kV detector panel movement demand from either of
the kV detector arm hand held controllers.
There are also three local feedback signals from the isolated side of the XIM. A kV and an MV and
held controller output feedback signal are active when there is an active digital output on the MV
and kV digital outputs respectively. Finally there is local feedback of the Linac ASU output which
indicates if there is current flowing through the Linac ASU output transistor (indicating that the
relays which hold down the ASU and enable button within the FKP are active).
A socketed CPLD provides a host communication watchdog and gating function for the digital
outputs. If a failure mode occurs, all the digital outputs are disabled by the hardware. Outputs
may also all be disabled with a jumper link. In addition, if the microprocessor reset signal is
asserted, the digital outputs will be disabled.
XVI R5.0x Document ID: 1522605_06
Corrective Maintenance Manual
© Elekta Group 2021. All rights reserved. Page 107

Product Description
Description of the Imaging Breakout Assembly
The Isolated Kilo Volt Arm Longitudinal/Lateral coarse/Fine A & Fine B/ Reference
(ISOL_KV_ACB_LONG /LAT_CRS/FINA/FINB/REF) contain the position information of the kV Arm.
Similarly the Isolated Meg Volt Arm Longitudinal/Lateral coarse/Fine A & Fine B/Reference
(ISOL_MV_ACB_LONG /LAT_CRS/FINA/FINB/REF) contain the position information of the MV Arm.
Digital inputs from the two arms contain the status of the manual brake and pivot open/closed
microswitches (kV/MV PIVOT_OPEN,PIVOT_CLOSED, MAN_BRK_REL, MBR_LED)
The Analogue Inputs (COLLIMATOR_CASS, FILTER_CASS) identify the different Collimator/Filter
cassettes. Each cassette is identified with a unique value of resistance which is measured with a
resistor divider. Additionally, CASSETTE_FITTED digital input has been provided to detect that
both the filter and collimator cassettes are fitted correctly.
An analogue input is provided to indicate if the KV source extended arm is extended. Finally there
is provision for future I/O needs in the form of a 15Way digital D type connector and 2 IDC
headers which are not populated.
4.20 Description of the Imaging Breakout Assembly
The Imaging Breakout Assembly is designed to support multiple functions in the system by
breaking into various signals and asserting some of them via relays.
It enables the Extended Imaging Module (XIM) to control MV and kV arm movements by breaking
into the HHC signals and routing them to the digital outputs of the XIM, allowing the XIM to
effectively press buttons on the HHC. Remote MV and kV arm movements can only occur when
authorized by both the function key pad via relays (FKP) and the XIM. If a button is pressed on the
HHC then XIM controlled movements are immediately disabled.
IBA supports remote panel retraction of the MV detector arm from the FKP. This is only supported
if the appropriate link plug is connected to SK60IBA1 instead of the XIM Digital IO connection
(1007653). It does this by asserting the MV and kV panel retract lines and the MV or kV user
enable lines. If a HHC demand button is pressed, remote movements are immediately disabled.
(See the iViewGT™ Corrective Maintenance Manual for more information).
It also routes the kV enable signal from the FKP to the kV generator footswitch input to let kV
radiation to be initiated from the FKP during a kV volume scan, to let the footswitch to be
removed.
In addition, the IBA supports kV gating from the FKP or external gating input, which sends a gating
request to the XVI control cabinet, through the XIM to the kV DCB (which will interrupt the trigger
pulses). Initiating a KV gate also switches off the kV room lamps. The gating signal path from the
external input to the kV DCB has a minimal delay to prevent unnecessary exposures.
The IBA also routes gantry speed relay signals from the gantry speed switch box to the gantry
speed module to allow variable speed for 4D image acquisition.
4.21 Description of the kV flexmap
4.21.1 Description of the flexmap calibration
A flexmap calibration is completed in a clockwise and counterclockwise direction for each FOV. Six
flexmap files are created.
• Small FOV
• Medium FOV
• Large FOV.
Document ID: 1522605_06 XVI R5.0x
Corrective Maintenance Manual
Page 108 © Elekta Group 2021. All rights reserved.

Product Description
Description of the kV flexmap
The VolumeView™ acquisition makes sure that the related flexmap file for each FOV and direction
of rotation is available for correction. PlanarView™ and MotionView™ acquisitions use the default
specified flexmap files in the SRI.ini file. All changes to a flexmap file are recorded in the log
files.
4.21.2 Changes to kV flexmap calibration for XVI R5.0 systems
The issue with the XVI systems before R5.0 is that these do not take account of Clockwise and
Counter Clockwise rotation directions so it is possible the best compensation is not necessarily
being applied. In XVI R5.0 it is necessary to have both Clockwise and Counter Clockwise flexmaps
present before PlanarView and MotionView acquisitions.
A clockwise and counter clockwise flexmap file must exist and not be further than one week
apart. The program will warn the user if the flexmaps:
• are more than one week apart
• CW and CCW flexmaps differ by more than 1mm from each other
If the flexmaps are older than 1 year a warning will appear or if older than 1.25 years an inhibit
will appear.

Troubleshooting
Important information before you start to do service work on the XVI system
5.1 Important information before you start to do service work
on the XVI system
WARNING 5.1
Ionizing radiation is hazardous to health. Do not go into the treatment room while the radiation
warning lamp near the treatment room door is on (illuminated). If you ignore this warning, it
can cause fatal injury.
CAUTION 5.1
The X-ray tube can be damaged if it is used at continuous high power levels for long periods.
The kV generator monitors the temperature of the kV source and prohibits radiation if the
temperature exceeds approximately 85°C. But, the heat capacity of the kV housing is greater than
that of the anode of the X-ray tube. Consequently the anode may overheat before the maximum
temperature of the kV source reaches 85°C.
Instead of having an independent XVI HHC and an independent iViewGT HHC, the Harmony
machine has an integrated HHC which can control the extending, retracting and lateral (small and
large FOV) movement of the arm. The integrated HHC of Harmony cannot control the center
panel movement, the pivot open movement and the pivot close movement.
The table below shows the corresponding relationship between the buttons of the XVI HHC (non-
Harmony) and the integrated HHC (Harmony). For example, the Open button on the XVI HHC
(non-Harmony) and the CENTER PANEL button on the integrated HHC (Harmony) have the same
function. In the following trouble shooting, instruction to press the Open button means press the
CENTER PANEL button on the integrated HHC.
XVI HHC (non-Harmony) Integrated HHC (Harmony)
Longitudinal movement button (G-direction) Extend
Longitudinal movement button (T-direction) Retract
Lateral movement button (large FOV direction) L/-Xr of panel movement controls
Lateral movement button (small FOV direction) S/+Xr of panel movement controls
Close button N/A
Open button CENTER PANEL button
5.2 Preparing to do service work on the kV source arm
WARNING 5.2
Before you do work in the treatment and equipment room in the path of the kV source arms,
obey the precautions given below. If you ignore the precautions, serious injury to you and other
persons and damage to the equipment can occur.
1 Extend the kV source to the locked position and attach a cable tie around the locking
mechanism of the kV source.
2 Put a note on the lock switch with the message that follows; Servicing in
operation. Do not retract the kV source arm!.

5.3 Preparing to do service activities on the detector panel
Before you go to the site where you do the troubleshooting
1 Print out the XVI kV detector panel request form and take the form with you.
You can get the form from this link, which goes to http://www.elektamarketing.info/
WebCommonFiles/Service/Request Forms/Image Guided Radiation Therapy/XVI Panel
Request form.pdf.
2 If you make changes to the XVI system during the troubleshooting procedure, do the
procedure Doing a test of the 3D image quality.
5.4 Completing service activities for the kV source assembly
1 Remove the cable tie from the locking mechanism of the kV source.
2 Remove the warning note from the lock switch.
WARNING 5.3
The kV source can be retracted at this time if necessary. Before you try to retract the kV source,
make sure there are no persons or objects in the area between the kV source and the gantry
drum of the digital accelerator. If you ignore this warning, there is a risk of injury to persons
and damage to equipment.
3 Make sure that the kV source arm interlock operates correctly.
5.5 Description of the XVI inhibits
When there is an inhibit on the XVI system, the inhibit status indicator has an orange background.
The inhibits in the table below are listed in the order in which they appear.
Table 5.1 XVI inhibits
Inhibit Description
Not Initialized The XVI system cannot start the kV radiation, because the kV
generator is not in the correct state.
HV Tank Pressure Switch The high voltage tank in the kV generator is defective.
CAN 1 Bus XVI CAN 1 bus (kV CAN) is not online.
CAN 2 Bus XVI CAN 2 bus (MV CAN) is not online.
kV DCB The XVI system cannot communicate with the kV DCB.
kV Imaging board There is possibly a problem with the power supply to the kV detector
panel.
Frame Rate The frame rate of the kV detector panel is incorrect.
kV Collimator Node The kV collimator node is not online.
Imaging Module Ref V The reference voltage for the imaging module is below the permitted
level.
Imaging Module 1 There is no communication between the imaging module and the XVI
Imaging Module 2 control cabinet.
Imaging Module 3
Imaging Module 4

Description of the XVI inhibits
Inhibit Description
Automated Linac ASU 1 A system check of the system that controls the automatic gantry
Automated Linac ASU 2 rotation was not successful.
Automated Linac ASU 3
Automated Linac ASU 4
kV Panel Lat The XVI system cannot communicate with the lateral node of the kV
detector panel.
kV Panel Long The XVI system cannot communicate with the longitudinal node of
the kV detector panel.
kV Panel Lateral Fault The lateral axis of the kV detector panel has a fault.
kV Panel Long Fault The voltage difference between the longitudinal fine A and fine B
potentiometers are not as expected.
kV Panel Lat Ref V The reference voltage for the lateral axis of the kV detector panel is
below the permitted level.
kV Panel Long Ref V The reference voltage for the longitudinal axis of the kV detector

Not calibrated One or more of the calibration settings for the position of the kV

Preset Rules Version The sri.ini and PresetRules.ini files do not have the same version of
the preset rules.
Gantry Node The gantry node is not online. The XVI control cabinet did not receive
the time stamp of the gantry angle from the kV detector control
board (DCB).
TCS Software Node The TCS software node is not online. There is possibly a problem with
the connection between the treatment control system and XVI.
Table The treatment table node is not online. There is possibly a problem
with the connection between the TCS and the XVI system.
TCS Software Release The software release of the treatment control system is not
compatible with this release of XVI.
kV Control Hardware A system check of the system that controls the kV radiation was not
successful.
kV Source Arm The kV source arm is not locked in the extended position, which is
necessary for kV image acquisition.
kV Panel Long Position The kV detector panel is not in a correct position longitudinally.
Reset Motors The XVI system cannot deliver kV radiation, because there is a
movement inhibit on the linear accelerator.
X-ray Tube Warm up The X-ray tube is not warmed up.
Protocol Dose The NominalScanDose of the VolumeView™ preset is more than the
CTDIVolNotification or CTDIVolAlert of the related protocol preset.
Room Doors The treatment room doors are not fully closed.
X-ray Tube Overload There is an X-ray tube overload.
X-ray tube is too hot The X-ray tube thermostat is activated (open circuit), because the X-
ray tube housing is too hot, or there is a thermostat malfunction.

Troubleshooting
Correcting the XVI system inhibit status
Inhibit Description
kV Generator Settings The kV generator did not accept one or more of the kV generator
settings that are specified in a preset (kV, mA, or ms).
Out of Tolerance One or more parameters are set out of the permitted ranges in the
acquisition preset. Red out of tolerance markers on the monitor for
the XVI control system and the XVI TRM show these parameters.
Settings Not Confirmed You did not click Confirm Settings to accept the preset settings.
kV Offset Calibration A kV offset calibration is in operation. This occurs automatically at
intervals of 35 s when the kV radiation is not on. XVI also does not do
a kV offset calibration during MV beam delivery to prevent MV
scattered radiation in the kV offset image. The XVI system completes
the calibration in a maximum time of two minutes. This inhibit will
also possibly appear after the acquisition of a series of kV images.
MV DCB XVI cannot communicate with the MV DCB. XVI uses data from the
MV DCB to sense when the MV radiation is on. When the MV
radiation is on, XVI does not do a kV offset calibration.
5.6 Correcting the XVI system inhibit status
XVI system inhibits and kV generator status appear in the lower, middle section of the XVI main

(1) in Figure 5.1 shows the inhibit status indicator. It gives information on the current inhibit
status of the XVI system. A double-click on the inhibit status indicator shows a full list of the
inhibits. The sequence of this list and the sequence in which the inhibits must be cleared, can be
different.
(2) in Figure 5.1 shows the kV generator status indicator. It gives information on the current
status of the kV generator. (see kV generator status).

Correcting the XVI system inhibit status
2 Make sure that you use the generator reset on the CITB to reset the generator.
3 Make sure that the cable between CITB and generator is connected and works correctly.
5.6.2 Correcting the HT tank pressure switch inhibit fault
1 For the Sedecal SHF435 DSI Series generator, make sure that ATP SW3-4 is in the OFF
position.
2 Make sure that the HT tank pressure switch terminals are correctly connected.
3 If the HT tank pressure switch inhibit continues, do the steps that follow.
a Disconnect the terminals from the HT tank pressure switch.
b Measure the electrical continuity across the pressure switch.
c If the pressure switch is closed circuit, it is defective.
The kVGenerator.log file shows an Interlock 4 Triggered entry.
4 Replace the unserviceable HT tank (see the Sedecal documentation supplied with the
equipment).
5.6.3 Correcting the Imaging Module Ref Voltage inhibit
A part of the XIM is defective. The reference voltage that the extended imaging module supplies
to the PCB of the collimator cassette, or the kV source extend switch is more than 0.1 V from its
nominal value.
1 Disconnect SK13A6 and SK13A7 temporarily for this measurement.
2 Examine the reference voltage, measure across PL13A6-1 and PL13A6-2 on the XIM.
This value must be 4.096 V.
– If the value is more than ± 0.1 V, replace the XIM.
An alternative measurement point is SK160kVS1-1 and SK160kVS1-2. It is the 5-way Molex
connector on the collimator assembly under the kV source arm covers.
3 Make sure that the XIM is energized and all connections are good.
– If the XIM is not energized, replace the XIM.
– If the CAN communication was lost, replace the XIM.
5.6.4 Correcting the CAN 1 Bus inhibit
This inhibit appears because the XVI CAN 1 bus (kV CAN) is not online
1 Make sure that the CB14 (iView arm) circuit breaker at the interface cabinet is in the ON
position.
2 Make sure that the CAN 1 cable is connected to the CAN 1 port on the XVI control cabinet.
3 Make sure that all the CAN cable connections from the XVI control cabinet to the interface
cabinet, to Area 13, to Area 16 and the kV DCB are connected correctly.
4 Make sure that the CAN PCB is correctly installed in the correct slot of the XVI control
cabinet and the dip switches set correctly.
5 Restart the XVI control cabinet.
6 Make sure that the CAN device software drivers are installed correctly.

5.6.5 Correcting the CAN 2 Bus inhibit
This inhibit appears because the XVI CAN 2 bus (kV CAN) is not online
1 Make sure that the IVIEW ARM and the circuit breaker in the interface cabinet is switched
on.
2 Make sure that the CAN 2 cable is connected to the CAN 2 port on the XVI control cabinet.
3 Make sure that all the CAN cable connections from the XVI control cabinet to iViewGT™

5.6.35 Correcting the kV control hardware inhibits
If a kV Control Hardware inhibit is displayed during SLG/MLG or filter calibration then unconfirm
and confirm the settings again.
If this inhibit appears, but not during SLG/MLG or filter calibration, do the procedures that follow.
The kV gating signals (KV_GATE or FKP_KV_GATE) are active before the acquisition starts.
Before you start an image acquisition, the kV control system does self-tests on the subassemblies
that follow:
• kV Generator (kV generator adaptor and interface PCBs)
• Imaging Breakout Assembly (IBA)
• Function keypad (FKP).
If a test is not successful, the system gives the kV control hardware inhibit which prevents the
radiation delivery.
The Extended Imaging Module (XIM) does tests on the kV control circuit. If a test is not successful,
the system gives the kV control hardware inhibit which prevents the radiation delivery.
1 On the TCS, set the machine state to Initialized.
2 Set the machine state to Preparatory.
The kV generator starts again. The kV generator causes the kV Radiation on LED (FKP) to
come on momentarily during the start up. This shows, that the kV generator and the FKP
communicate.
3 On the FKP, make sure that the kV radiation on LED comes on momentarily when the kV
generator starts.
This shows there is successful communications between the kV generator and FKP.
4 If the kV radiation on LED (FKP) does not come on, examine the signal path cables between
the kV generator and FKP.
5 Examine the 24V DC supply from the kV Generator, measure between pins SK98E2-33 and
SK98E2-34.
6 On the IBA, press the KV GATE TEST button.
The kV Gate LED comes on.
7 Use the XIM Utility to examine the interface with the XIM.
Make sure that the signals Ext kV gate and FKP kV Gate are green. See the XIM Utility.
8 On the IBA, press the KV GATE TEST button.
The signals Ext kV gate and FKP kV Gate change to a gray color.
9 Refer to the log file XIMDigitalInputs.log where changes to these two signals are recorded.
10 If the signals do not change color, examine the cables between the IBA and XIM.
11 Make sure that the XIM is energized and all the connections are correct.

5.6.36 Examining the kV enable dropped inhibit
If during kv Tube warm-up or SLG/MLG calibration, a kV enable dropped inhibit occurs, it is
because the user pressed the expose button on FKP before the generator state goes to standby.
Do the kV tube warm-up or SLG/MLG calibration again. Press the expose button after the
generator state goes into standby.
If this inhibit has caused a volume scan to terminate, and is out of tolerance, this is recorded in
the InhibitManager.log and acqEngine.log file.
1 Start a volume scan and change the isocentric or column rotation from 0º to 2º.
2 Make sure that the scan terminates when the kV enable dropped message appears
that shows the cause is Out of Tolerance and that the correct table axis is identified
(isocentric or column rotation).
Note: Out of tolerance messages will appear for the relative and absolute movement of the column and
isocentric axes.
5.6.37 Correcting the MV DCB inhibit
XVI cannot communicate with the MV detector control board (DCB). XVI uses data from the MV
DCB to sense when the MV radiation is on. When the MV radiation is on, XVI does not do a kV
offset calibration.
Also MV radiation on signal is also used to correct pulse line artifacts when performing
intrafraction imaging.
1 Restart the XVI control cabinet.
2 Make sure that the CAN 2 (MV bus) cable is connected to the CAN 2 port on the XVI control
cabinet.
3 Make sure that all CAN 2 cable connections from the XVI control cabinet to the iViewGT™
control cabinet to the MV DCB to XIM are connected and not defective.
4 Make sure that there is power to the MV DCB.
5 Make sure that the CAN PCB is correctly installed in the correct slot of the XVI control
cabinet and the DIP switches are correctly set.
6 Make sure that the software drivers for the CAN device are installed correctly.
7 If you do not have iViewGT installed on your system you will have to disable the MV DCB
inhibit. To disable the MV DCB inhibit, do as follows:
a Open the sri.ini file.
b Find the [MVDCB] section and make sure that Disable=1.

5.8.1.7 Correcting the Acquisition ended during irradiation error
NOT TO SCALE 002813 ©2011 Elekta Limited
Figure 5.15 Message error - Acquisition ended during irradiation
1 Go to the ContextManager.log , look which parameter is out of tolerance (OOT) and
examine the cause.
Possible causes are:
– The table position went out of tolerance during irradiation.
– The table position tolerance values in the sri.ini file were set too low. In particular, the
parameter TableLinearToleranceMM in the service settings of the sri.ini file should
not be changed to a value of less than the default value.
– Electrical noise is affecting the table position values.
Note: An Out of Tolerance inhibit on the XVI main display is also shown at the same time with this error
message (see Correcting the XVI inhibits).
2 Examine the cables between the XIM and the IBA and the cable between the IBA and the
FKP.
3 Look at the sri.log file for the Date & time ActivatedkVEnableInterlock and the
message.
4 Look at the InhibitManager.log file for the Date & time
ActivatedkVEnableInterlock and the message.
5 If the message shows that the kV enable signal dropped, make sure that the cable from the
ICCA to the kV generator is not defective.
6 Look at the kVGenerator.log file or the ContextManager.log file for the Date & time
(Interlock1 kV distributed/Enable) Triggered and the message.
7 Make sure that the touchguard was not triggered.
8 Examine the log files kVpanelLat.log and kVpanelLong.log.
9 Monitor the longitudinal (Long) and lateral (Lat) coarse and fine signals with the XIM
program.
When the panel does not move the signals do not change by more than ±5 mV.
10 If the signals change by more than ±5 mV, Make sure that the cables 1006177 and 1006178
are routed away from the LV PSU (see Removing the XIM CAN input cable and Installing the
XIM CAN input cable).
11 Do a check of the longitudinal drive mechanism.
12 If it is necessary, correct mechanical instability (see Correcting the mechanical instability
error)..
13 Make sure that the tension of the longitudinal drive belt is correct (see Removing the
longitudinal drive belts and Installing the longitudinal drive belts).
14 Set the correct flex midpoint offset (see Doing the manual calibration procedure to correct
the flex midpoint offset).

5.8.1.9 Correcting the Flexmap shows error: not enough projection images message
Message Flexmap shows error: not enough projection images.
Messages in the sri.log file are: ct_frame_buffer
flush timed out, Volume View: frames acquired ###"
to be less than KVPanel.log "Sent triggers ###
Flexmap images are captured at 1024 × 1024 pixels and therefore the buffer fills up faster than
usual scans.
1 Restart the XVI control cabinet and do the Flexmap calibration again.
2 Make sure that the XVI control cabinet operates correctly.
3 Do a scan of the hard disk drive.
4 Do a defragmentation.
5 Make sure that not more than 5 multilevel gains were completed.
The time to process and write each image to the hard disk drive increases, when more than
5 multilevel gain files are used. This decrease in performance will cause the buffer to
overflow.
6 Make sure that Flexmaps calibration can be completed.

5.9 XVI software faults
5.9.1 Correcting the security event log is full error
Message The security event log is full and only an
administrative user can correct the problem.
1 Start as follows to change the event log settings.
2 Log on as the administrator.
3 Select Start > Run.
4 Type eventvwr into the Run dialog box and click the OK button.
The Event Viewer window opens.
5 Right-click the Security menu and select the Properties option.
6 Change the Maximum log size to 8192 kB (8 MB).
7 Make sure that the Overwrite events as needed option is selected.
8 Click the Clear Log button.
You will be asked if you want to save the logs.
9 Click the Yes button to save the security event logs to file.
10 Close all windows and log off.
This completes the changes to the event log settings.
11 Make sure that you can log on as the XVI user.

Troubleshooting
XVI software faults
5.9.2 Correcting the No Windows Start button on the control room
monitor fault
Symptom There is no Windows Start button on the control room monitor (XVI
windows), but it is on the treatment room monitor.
1 Make sure that the cables are connected to the correct ports on the back of the XVI control
cabinet.
2 Press CTRL + ALT + DEL to bring up the task manager.
3 End the trmdriver.exe process.
4 Move the mouse to the treatment room monitor and the Start button back to the control
room monitor.
5 Restart the XVI program.
This restarts the trmdriver.exe process.
6 Make sure that the Start button is on the control room monitor.
5.9.3 Correcting the wrong gantry angle shown in the TRM
Symptom The gantry angle shown on the XVI TRM and the treatment control system
(TCS) TRM is not the same..
1 Move the gantry to different points around the gantry arc.
2 Make sure that the gantry angle shown on the XVI TRM and the treatment control system
(TCS) TRM is the same.
3 If the gantry angles are different, Use the DCB utility program to type the gantry calibration
values again.
5.9.4 Correcting the No HexaPOD option in "Correction by" drop down list
in the reference viewer
Symptom In reference viewer the option for HexaPOD is not in the "Correction by"
drop down list, but a Hexapod table is installed.
1 In the [Table] section of the sri.ini file set as follows: Hexapod = 1.
5.9.5 Correcting the XVI does not respond error
Symptom XVI does not respond.
The XVI software does not respond when the MV CAN bus server restarts.
1 Close the XVI program, use the Task Manager if necessary.
2 Make sure that the MV CAN server operates and all CAN cables are correctly connected.
3 Restart the XVI program.
XVI R5.0x Document ID: 1522605_06
Corrective Maintenance Manual
© Elekta Group 2021. All rights reserved. Page 161

Troubleshooting
XVI software faults
5.9.6 Correcting the error when XVI closes unexpectedly
Symptom The XVI system closes unexpectedly.
This can occur after an upgrade of the XVI software.
1 Make sure that all parameters in the sri.ini file are in the correct format.
2 Make sure that all parameters only contain letters, numbers or a combination of the two.
3 Make sure that you have typed the parameter correctly.
4 Make sure that the XVI control cabinet is connected to an active network.
5.9.7 Correcting the fault where the user cannot login to the XVI software
Symptom Input fields greyed out. Can occur after a dialogue message appears and
disappears before it can be acknowledged.
XVI is waiting for user input on a hidden dialogue and therefore appears to be unresponsive.
1 Use the XVI desktop icon and start the XVI program again.
2 Close the XVI program with the Task Manager.
3 Restart the XVI program and confirm all messages.
5.9.8 Correcting Brightness and Contrast scroll bars are disabled
Symptom: Can not change brightness and contrast as the Brightness and Contrast scroll bars are
disabled.
Resolution: This can happen when the AutoBC is ticked when doing a movie playback.
1 Do a movie playback on a VolumeView™ image with AutoBC not selected.
2 Click Done.
The scrollbars will now be active and you can change the brightness and contrast.
5.9.9 Correcting a gantry does not rotate during a One-Touch
VolumeView™ acquisition fault
This fault can occur if the calibration of the gantry movement speed was not completed.
1 In Service Mode, click the Service Functions.
2 Click Edit Machine Item Parts.
3 In the Edit Machine Item Parts window, select Item 370 Part 159.
4 Do not make an entry in the Set box, which sets the value of Item 370 Part 159 to 0.
5 Click Deliver Stored Beam.
6 In the Deliver Stored Beam window, in the Beams list, select kV FlexMap Cal.
7 Click the Radiation tab.
8 In the Energy list, select an applicable energy.
9 Click the Geometry tab.
10 Set the Gantry Angle to -150°.
11 Use gantry ASU to clear out-of-tolerance settings.
Document ID: 1522605_06 XVI R5.0x
Corrective Maintenance Manual
Page 162 © Elekta Group 2021. All rights reserved.

Troubleshooting
XVI software faults
12 Set the Gantry Angle to 150°.
13 Start gantry ASU and record the time of rotation in seconds.
14 Use the equation that follows to calculate the gantry speed gain.
Gantrys speed gain = Arc rotation time (s) × (Item 148 Part138 × 2048)/18000
Note: If the calculated value contains decimal numbers, round it to the nearest whole number.
15 In the Edit Machine Item Parts window, set Item 370 Part 137 to the value of the gantry
speed gain.
16 On the primary function toolbar, click Save to open the Save Linac Database and CalBlocks
window.
17 Click Save LINAC Calibration to save the gantry speed calibration.

5.14.2 Correcting the error RPS UID (xxxxxx) Can't be found
This error can occur when you try to do distributed imaging. The workflow manager on the
Sequencer communicates with XVI through Microsoft Message Queue (MSMQ) about RPS data
that XVI must receive. XVI shows this error if it does not receive RPS data. The error can occur
because:
• The location of dicomtransitdb was changed but the XVIDicomSCP service was not
restarted.
• RPS data was not sent to XVI through DICOM because the XVIDicomSCP service was
not run or if RAW_DATA service was not set up in mergecom.app.
1 Use the table that follows to correct the error:
If Then
The location of dicomtransitdb was changed but the Restart the XVIDicomSCP

was not set up in the mergecom.app. mergecom.app.
5.14.3 Correcting the DICOM import failure
A parameter in sri.ini intended for diagnostic use can cause the DICOM import to fail with no
error messages reported on the third party system or in the XVI DICOM logs for the transfer. The
patient data is not imported into the XVI database. The DICOM data files are not transferred to
the Dicomtransitdb folder but the files can be found in another folder, for example c:\temp.
1 Make sure that the parameter MsglogDirectory under the [Dicom] section in the sri.ini file
is empty (blank).
2 Restart the XVIDicomSCP for the change to take effect.
Note: The MsglogDirectory parameter is used to capture failed transfer messages to the XVIDicomSCP
and therefore should only be used as a temporary diagnostic setting. Setting a directory path for
the MsglogDirectory parameter will cause all DICOM data to be directed to the MsglogDirectory
irrespective of whether the transfer to the XVIDicomSCP was successful or not. The XVIDicomSCP
does not try to validate the messages and does nothing more with them.

5.14.4 Correcting the fault where data is listed under different patient
Sending DICOM patient data to XVI results in certain data being listed under a different patient.
For example, the full patient data set is sent to XVI (RT plan, RT structure set, and reference CT
images). However, one or more of the data objects is listed as a different patient.
1 In the treatment planning system, make sure that these DICOM tags for each of the DICOM
objects are identical:
– Patient ID
– Patient Name
– Date of Birth
– Sex.

6 Make sure that the MOSAIQ DCMWIN server is running and not reporting any errors.
7 Do a check for the MOSAIQ DCMWIN server mergecom.app to make sure that the
following services are supported.
– SERVICE_<number> = SPATIAL_REGISTRATION
– SERVICE_<number> = RAW_DATA
5.14.11 Correcting the DICOM Raw Data storage service is not supported by
server <AE title> error
1 Configure <AE title> in mergecom.app or disable Distributed solution error
message.
2 In the SRI.ini file, make sure that the correct AE_title is set in [DICOM]
DistributedSolutionAETitle=
3 In the mergecom.app file, make sure that the DCMwin configured service list contains:
– SERVICE_<number> = SPATIAL_REGISTRATION
– SERVICE_<number> = RAW_DATA
5.14.12 RPS data exported from XVI but RPS data not appearing in
MOSAIQ®
1 Make sure there are no DICOM related errors.
2 In MOSAIQ® make sure that the Dicom Import Queue is being processed at File > Import/
Export > DICOM Import Queue. Only items in the Bookmarked state should be listed.
3 On the MOSAIQ® server, stop and start the Mosaiq Work Queue service.

5.15.1 Analyzing the log files
The log files that are most important for fault analysis (excluding DICOM problems) are:
• acqEngine.log
• ServiceCalibration.log
• AcquisitionDlg.log
• sri.log
• ContextManager.log
• InhibitManager.log
• KVGenerator.log
• KVPanel.log.
• kVDCB.log
To do a log file analysis:
1 Examine three or four log files to find the cause of a problem. When an entry related to a
fault is found in one log file, other log files can be examined for entries that occurred at
approximately the same time.
Entries to look for are:
XVI R5.0x Document ID: 1522605_06
Corrective Maintenance Manual
© Elekta Group 2021. All rights reserved. Page 193

Troubleshooting
Description of the XVI log files
– Error messages: Words to look for are ERROR and WARNING.
– Out of tolerance messages that are not cleared. The ContextManager.log uses OOT
and InTOL to report parameters going out of and in to tolerance. The
InhibitManager.log uses Activated:[OutOfTolerance] and Cleared:
[OutOfTolerance].
– Nodes that change their status unusually, or go off-line and do not go back to on-line.
– Beam On and Beam Off events and associated triggers.
Note: There can be other words in the different log files to indicate an error. For example the
DicomSCP.log and the DicomSCU.log use MC3. The easiest way to examine a log file is with the
Edit > Find function and the word error.
– In the acqEngine.log look out for a log:
-04-19 17:37 [error] kVSequencer - Missing kv frame
(xxxxxx)
This is a normal log in the start/stop motion view and is just indicating an unexpected
difference between the previous radiated frame number and the current frame
number. The frame number is different because the motion view was paused and
started again.
Refer to the Sample log messages. It shows the messages from the log files during a typical
XVI program start up and image acquisition.

5.15.3 Log file descriptions
This section gives a description of the primary log files and examples of error messages.
5.15.3.1 XVI sri.log
The sri.log is generated by the program SRI.exe. This log reports the collection of images and 3-D
image reconstructions. The sri.log also reports the regular update of the KV offset image. Before
each Beam on, the KV offset update is stopped.
sri.log error messages

Initialise
These messages are logged when the kV detector panel does not communicate with the XVI
control cabinet. The cause could be a cable problem or a defective PSU.

5.15.3.2 Examining the Context Manager.log
The ContextManager.log is generated by the program ContextMgr.exe. The Context
Manager examines that the inhibits are as expected and values are within tolerance for a
particular context. For example, the kV generator requires a number of conditions for a tube
warm-up. The context manager examines these values and instructs the Inhibit Manager whether
they are satisfactory or not.
1 Look in this log for parameters going out of tolerance (OOT) that do not subsequently go in
tolerance (InTol).
The parameter name indicates the item that went out of tolerance, for example:
OOT[LateralTableShift]
5.15.3.3 Examining the InhibitManager.log
The InhibitManager is a software object residing in the program ContextMgr.exe. At XVI start
up, the InhibitManager.log lists a series of [...NodeNotOnline] messages. As each
node goes on line the InhibitManager.log reports the NotOnLine message as being
cleared. Many of these messages relate to CAN nodes that are not yet supported.
1 Look in InhibitManager.log for inhibits that are Activated that are not
subsequently Cleared.
Typical error messages in the InhibitManager.log are:

5.15.3.4 KVGenerator.log
The KVGenerator.log is generated by the program KVGenerator.log. The
KVGenerator.log reports changes of status. Typical status messages are:
Status change => SafeWait[0]
Status change => Standby[1]
Status change => Preparatory[2]
Status change => XRay[3]
The KVGenerator.log periodically reports the heat units (HU).
For example:
Anode: Energy=50.9 kJ, 12.0%, Body: Energy=5.3 kJ,
0.6%, HU=12.0%, Sedecal HU=10
Note: The heat units logged in KVGenerator.log are derived from a different software model to the
heat units displayed on the XVI monitor and there is a small difference between the two values.
The KVGenerator.log periodically reports the total time that the system has radiated since
the XVI control cabinet was installed. Note that software recovery may reset this value.
Tube Usage Hours= 19.06, Overloads = 27, Avoidable = 0
The KVGenerator.log reports commands to the kV generator to load new preset values:
*cmd: SetupPF(kV=80, mA=40, mS=10) - The warm-up preset is loaded.
SetAttribkV() : 80[kV] Setup Complete - The kV generator is ready.
KVGenerator.log error messages
Error messages that appear in KVGenerator.log in the format ERROR[nn]:... originate
on the kV generator. For a list of the kV generator error codes together with possible causes and
actions. Refer to the documentation of the generator manufacturer (Sedecal) that is supplied with
the kV generator.
For example: ERROR[33]: Generator Not Communicatingindicates there is no
communication between the kV generator and the XVI control cabinet.

5.15.3.5 Examining the KVDCB.log
The KVDCB.log records Beam On and Beam Off events and the number of trigger pulses sent.
When the message that follows appears before Beam On, it shows normal operation and can be
ignored:
Warning: not enough timelapse on consecutive frames
1 If this message occurs excessively, examine the CAN bus cables, connections and
terminations.
There can be a problem with the kV DCB or with the CAN bus panel trigger circuits.
5.15.3.6 DicomSCPStatus.log
The DicomSCPStatus.log is generated by XVI DICOM SCP. It contains basic information
about the SCP activity and validation errors. This is a useful log to inspect if a DICOM transfer is
failing between a DICOM third party and the XVI DICOM SCP.
5.15.3.7 DicomSCP.log
The DicomSCP.log is generated by the programming tool kit used by XVI DICOM SCP when
some data is received. This log contains details of the DICOM transaction as well as the type of
data pushed to XVI SCP. The log can be difficult to read as many of the messages are not relevant
to the user.
DicomSCP.log error messages
Errors are indicated by a line containingMC3 E:
(3632) 01-09 14:23:27.34 MC3 E: ...
For example:
(3632) 01-09 14:23:27.34 MC3(TL_Start_Transport_Listener) E:
winsock bind error: (-1) WSAGetLastError: (0) (3632) 01-09
14:23:27.34 MC3 E: UL Provider aborted the association (2344)
01-09 14:23:27.34 MC3(TL_Start_Transport_Listener) E: Failure
binding
The port that the SCP is trying to open is blocked by another program.
5.15.3.8 DICOMSCU.log
The DICOMSCU.log is generated by the programming toolkit used by the XVI DICOM SCP when
data is sent. This log contains details of the DICOM transaction as well as the type of data pushed
from XVI. Because the log is generated by the toolkit used by the SCP it can be difficult to read as
it contains a lot of information, much of which is not relevant to the user.
DicomSCU.log error messages
Errors are indicated by a line containingMC3 E:
(3632) 01-09 14:23:27.34 MC3 E: ...
For example:
(2148) 11-20 11:05:39.58 MC3 E: program title "DICOMAPP1" is not
configured
The program that XVI is trying to export to was configured in sri.ini but not in
mergecom.app.

5.16 Common faults in the kV generator log file
5.16.1 Correcting the workstation mismatch in the kV generator log file
If the message that follows is found in the kV generator log file:
ERROR: Workstation and Workingmode Mismatch [Workstation=PF(2),
Workingmode=Standard RF(4)] or similar [Workstation=PF(2),
Workingmode=Buckey 1(1)]
1 If your kV generator is an SHF 435, do the procedure that follows (see Configuring the kV
generator to run in Workstation 2 - DSI mode (WS=2) for the Sedecal SHF 435 DSI Series).
Note: The SHF 435 is not applicable for Harmony.
2 If your kV generator is an SHFR 400, do the procedure that follows (see Configuring the kV
generator to run in Workstation 2 - DSI mode (WS=2) for the Sedecal SHFR 400 DSI Series).
5.16.1.1 Configuring the kV generator to run in Workstation 2 - DSI mode (WS=2) for the
Sedecal SHF 435 DSI Series (non-applicable for Harmony)
1 Exit the XVI program and power off the generator.
2 On the ATP controller board, make sure that the DIP switch ATPSW2-3 is set to the ON
position.
This puts the kV generator in Service mode.
3 Switch on the kV generator and start the Sedecal Generator Service\Service Console
program.
4 Select Configuration.
5 Make sure that WS2 is selected as DSI and select WS2.
Note: WS1 is selected by default when the Sedecal Generator Service window opens.
6 Select Exit/OK to save the configuration and close the Sedecal Generator Service\Service
Consolewindow.
7 De-energize the kV generator and set the DIP switch ATPSW2-3 to the OFF position.
That puts the kV generator back to Clinical Mode.

1 Exit the XVI program and power off the generator.

Note: WS1 is selected by default when the Sedecal Generator Service window opens.
5 Select Exit/OK to save the configuration and close the Sedecal Generator Service\Service
Console window.
6 Make sure that the XVI program works without a generator fault.
If other messages than Workstation and Workingmode mismatch are reported
in the kVGenerator.log file. See to the Sedecal documentation supplied with the

5.16.2 Correcting the Cannot Open Port 1 error
If the Sedecal Generator Service program gives the error message:
Cannot Open Port 1
The fault could be:
• The XVI program was not closed before the Sedecal Generator Service\Service Console
program was run
• There is a communication problem between the XVI control cabinet and the kV
generator
• There is a problem with the HT controller or ATP console in the kV generator (Sedecal
SHF 435 DSI Series) or the Control Board (Sedecal SHFR 400 DSI Series)
Note: The Sedecal SHF 435 DSI Serires is non-appllicable for Hamrony.
• The port selection in the ServiceConsole.ini file in the Tech Service folder is incorrect.
1 Examine the KVGenerator.log file for the error messages given in Correcting the faults that
appear on the kV generator status indicator.
2 Do the given troubleshooting procedure.
Refer also to the documentation of the generator manufacturer (Sedecal) for more
troubleshooting.

5.16.3 Correcting the kV generator log message ERROR[50]((null)):
Exposure Aborted by the Operator
The error 50 in the kV generator log appears when the XRAYON signal is deactivated during the
first exposure.
1 Make sure that you have the correct firmware.

Revision = V07R07
– If you have the incorrect firmware, upgrade the EPROM firmware.
5.17 Correcting the kV source faults
5.17.1 Correcting the X-ray tube arcs error E09 (generator overload)
WARNING 5.4
Do not stand or sit on the kV source arms. If you ignore this warning, you can cause injury to
you, damage to the equipment and clinical mistreatment.
For the kV generator - Sedecal SHFR 400 DSI Series refer to section 4.15.3.4 KVGenerator.log for
log of generator overloads. Some X-ray tubes were found to arc after hard operation of the tube,
for example after tests at high kV and mS settings. A tube can recover if it is run at lower kV
settings for some days, but this will have an effect to the X-ray density and the low contrast
resolution of the images.
This will not be a problem for images that are registered using bony landmarks, but can be a
problem if soft tissue marks are being aligned.
1 If the clinical users agree, Adjust the presets to run the tube at 100 kV instead of 120 or 130
kV for some days.
2 Adjust the presets then to their original values again.
The X-ray tube can recover after that.
5.17.2 Touchguard inhibits shown by the TCS
This inhibit can occur on:
• kV detector arm
• MV detector arm
• Head touchguard ring
• kV source arm (non-applicable for Hamrony)

5.18 Correcting the kV detector arm and kV detector panel faults
5.18.1 Correcting a recently calibrated system displays inhibits that shows
the kV detector panel is not in the correct position
This can occur when the kV detector panel is incorrectly aligned or tightened after a servicing
procedure.
Examples:
• Replacement of the lateral drive assembly
• Replacement of the longitudital or lateral linear bearings
• Servicing work inside the kV detector arm during which it was necessary to loosen the
linear bearing block or interface block.
1 Do a check of the kV detector arm.
2 Do a check of the kV detector arm during rotation.
3 Make sure that the mechanical instability is correct.

5.18.2 Correcting a mechanical instability error
1 Make sure the lateral drive assembly and the two M6 screws are tight.
2 Use the table that follows to do the troubleshooting if there is a mechanical instability error
in the kV detector arm.
If Then
The lateral drive is loose 1 Remove the screws, and clean the screw threads.
2 Apply a thin layer of Loctite® 222 to the screw threads.
3 Install the screws.
The lateral drive is not 1 Make sure that the:
loose
‒ Interface block and bearing block align correctly.
‒ The torque setting of the fixings is correct.
2 If it is necessary, correct the faults found.
3 Do the calibrations and tests that follow:
a Calibrate the kV detector arm (hardware).
b Calibrate the kV detector arm (software).
c Calibrate the kV flexmaps.
d Make sure that the touchguard of each kV detector arm operates correctly.
e Do the test of the 3D image quality.
f Do the test of the registration accuracy of the 3D image.
g Do the test of the registration accuracy of the 2D image.
5.18.3 Correcting the ACB PSU failure
Damage to one or the two ACB PSUs is probable if the power cable and the sense cable (if
applicable) are not correctly installed. Systems with the combined XVI/iViewGT™ PSU chassis
have LED indicators on the +24V and the -24V PSU to show that the output supply is present.
1 Before you start the XVI control cabinet:
a Make sure that the power cable and the sense cable (if applicable) are correctly
installed.
b Make sure that the connectors are correctly terminated.
5.18.4 Correcting the audible alarm sound after you have replaced the PCB
from the arm control board
Symptom The system gives an audible alarm after the PCB of the ACB was replaced.
1 Do the procedure Calibrating the kV detector arm (Hardware).
The audible alarm stops when the PCB of the ACB is calibrated.

5.18.5 Correcting the noise from the kV detector arm during gantry
rotation
Symptom Noise (sound) from the kV detector arm while the gantry rotates.
Most probable this noise comes from the kV detector arm assembly counter balance weight. The
counter balance weight moves side ways during gantry rotation and the spring and the pin rub
against each other.
1 Rotate the gantry to 90°.
2 Remove the cover of the kV detector arm.
The actuator is visible now. The counter balance assembly is at the bottom.
3 Pull the counter balance weight in your direction and look inside.
You will see the spring that rubs against the pin.
4 Put some grease on the pin.
5.18.6 Correcting the circuit breaker CB14 switches off occasionally fault
1 Make sure that CB14 is a 10 A circuit breaker.
It is possible that the circuit breaker CB14 (iView arm) was not upgraded during the
installation of XVI.
5.18.7 Correcting the movement failure of the kV detector panel and kV
detector arm
The LEDs on the ACB help you to find faults in the kV detector panel movement system. The LEDs
are in NORMAL mode (jumper PL7 positioned to the right), when they are referred to unless
specified differently.
Also refer to section to the ACB diagnostics LEDs for more information.
Related Links:
ACB diagnostic LEDs on page 92

1 Examine the LEDs on the ACB column.

5.18.8 Correcting the middle arm when it does not fully extend or retract
Symptom The middle arm does not fully extend or retract. The brake lights flash.
This occurs while the middle arm is closed and the button on the HHC is released for a second.
One of the brakes on the longitudinal drive mechanism did not operate correctly. This could be an
indication that the ACB is in an unknown position.
1 Release the brake and move the cradle to the fully retracted position to re-establish a
known position.
2 Do a check of the longitudinal drive mechanism.
3 Make sure that the drive mechanism can move freely.
4 Correct mechanical faults in the mechanism.
5 If it is necessary, Replace the brake.
5.18.9 Correcting a noisy or loose pivot movement (non-applicable for
Hamrony)
During the pivot movement of the kV detector arm, rollers at the ends of the latch pins give
smooth and stable movement. The rollers move along and apply a force to the metal track.
Adjustment screws let you adjust the rollers, and therefore the force they apply to the track.
1 Set the gantry to 0°.
2 Move the kV detector arm to the center of its range of rotation.
That is approximately 45°, between the fully open and fully closed positions.
3 Adjust the pivot movement (see Figure 5.54).
a Loosen the lock nuts.
b If it is necessary, turn the adjustment screws clockwise to extend the latch pins to the
metal track.
CAUTION 5.2
Do not tighten the pivot adjustment screws too much. If the adjustment screws are too tight, it
is possible that the XVI system cannot move freely. If you ignore this caution, it can cause
damage to the equipment.
c If it is necessary, turn the adjustment screws counterclockwise to retract the latch
pins away from the metal track.
d When the movement is smooth and makes no noise, tighten the lock nuts.

In manual mode after the kV enable button is pressed on the FKP, the radiation lamp is on. After
one pulse the FKP goes to interrupt, but the radiation lamp stays on. No radiation is delivered.
The radiation lamp also stays on when the room door is opened.
Note: This is not dangerous, because no radiation is on. This only occurs in manual calibration mode.

5.23.1 Correcting the XVI control cabinet does not start from the recovery
CD
1 Restart the XVI control cabinet.
2 When the initial start up screen appears, hold down the DELETE key to open the BIOS
settings.
The BIOS is password protected, make sure that you have the password for the BIOS
settings.
3 Go to Advanced BIOS Features.
4 Change the First Boot Device to CDROM.
5 Change the Second Boot Device to HDD-0.
6 Press the ESC key.
7 Press the F10 key.
8 Press the Y key.
9 Press the ENTER key.
The computer will restart automatically and will boot from the recovery disk in the DVD
drive.
10 Follow the instructions on the screen to load the software again.
5.23.2 Correcting the excessive error frame rate on the CAN card in the XVI
control cabinet
If the error frame rate becomes excessive on the CAN card in the XVI control cabinet, the card
automatically withdraws from the bus. The card therefore stops receiving and routing CAN
messages, so all proxies are starved and consequently time out 5 seconds later. Each proxy then
goes offline and causes an inhibit.
In XVI, only three proxies use the CAN bus, therefore three inhibits occur:
• kV DCB (0x0001001)
• gantry (0x0001004)
• table (0x0001006).
This fault can occur when a new CAN device is added to the bus, or an existing CAN
device fails, therefore disturbing the bus termination.

cables between them.

– Green kV radiation off LED goes off.
5.24.6 Correcting a Radiation does not start fault
When you start to irradiate, after XVI is installed, and you cannot radiate, or you get one of these
symptoms, do this procedure to get the linear accelerator to operate correctly.
• There is no radiation
• There is no gantry movement
• There is no change in the generator status
• No inhibits or messages on the system.
1 On the HHC, press the kV START button.
2 Press the RESET MOTORS button to reset the linear accelerator.

5.31 Diagnosing image quality problems
5.31.1 Description of the image quality problems for an XVI system
This section gives information on problems related to the quality of kV images.
WARNING 5.6
Do not stand or sit on the kV source arms. If you ignore this warning, you can cause injury to
you, damage to the equipment and clinical mistreatment.
During the acquisition of kV images with the XVI system some quality problems can occur. Section
Correcting image symptoms- Spatial, contrast resolution and uniformity and section Correcting
image symptoms - Ring on images give possible causes on common types of bad quality kV
images and the applicable troubleshooting procedure.
Some of these problems cannot be resolved, because they are characteristic to the system (for
example radar artifacts, see Correcting image characteristics that cannot be resolved . In this
section we are trying to enhance your understanding of what can be resolved and what cannot.
As each individual bad quality problem is different, we can only give directions on how to do the
troubleshooting.
Document ID: 1522605_06 XVI R5.0x
Corrective Maintenance Manual
Page 248 © Elekta Group 2021. All rights reserved.

Troubleshooting
Diagnosing image quality problems
Cone-beam CT scanning is a two step process. The first step is the image acquisition followed by
the second step ‘reconstruction’. There are many parameters that can effect image quality. Some
of these are related to conditions in which the measurements are made to acquire the data.
Others are related to the computation and the mathematical process, other parameters concern
the display system itself.
An Artifact is a distortion that can occur in reconstructed images. Artifacts may have different
causes and appear in different ways. They may originate from the scanning system, the patient,
and the algorithm, or may be attributed to material outside the scanned field.
Movement artifacts are caused by movement of the patient or organs in the patient (breathing
and heart beating). These artifacts can be minimized by better immobilization of the patient and
by more rapid measurements (short bursts of radiation).
Movement artifacts cause blurring, if the motion is periodic and occur a large number of times
during the scan time.
Alignment artifacts are caused by the fact that the center of rotation of the imaging system is not
necessarily the same as the reconstruction matrix. The center of rotation produces a distortion in
the shape of the internal structures.
Ring artifacts appear as circles in the image. They are caused by a drift in the gain or offset, of
some of the detectors. The amplitude of the ring artifacts increases rapidly when the drifting
detector is close to the center of the array.
Noise and dose: If a homogenous material is scanned, it will be found that the CT numbers of a
given region are not all the same values, as would be expected. The attenuation values will be
randomly distributed around some average value. Noise is expressed in terms of standard
deviation, sigma, of attenuation coefficients. Noise depends on the size of the object, the kV, the
pixel size, the slice thickness and the skin dose.
Contrast resolution can be defined as the ability of a system to distinguish details from their
surrounding structures, while the relative contrast of those details to that of adjacent structures is
low. It is assumed in this definition, that the size of the details to be detected is large enough so
as not to constitute a limiting factor in the ability of the system to detect them.
Spatial resolution refers to the ability of a system to distinguish small details from their
surrounding structures. In this definition, it is assumed that the relative contrast of those details
to the surrounding structures is high enough so as not to constitute a limiting factor in the ability
of the system to detect them.
It is expected that the smallest object visible would be equal to the pixel size. In practice however
the smallest discernible detail is somewhat larger.
The following parameters can have a degrading effect on spatial resolution:
• Artifacts
• Non-adequate convolution filter
• Scattered radiation.
5.31.2 What is a radar artifact?
This is a semi-circular shape in a CBCT scan which looks like a radar screen image. It is not
uniform, the brightness decays in the CBCT because of the acquisition time. It is a disk and not a
ring.

5.31.3 What is the cause of an radar artifact?
The cause of the radar artifact can be because of the panel lag. When an image is grabbed, the
image does not disappear from the panel instantaneously, it fades gradually and produces a ghost
image. The ghost image will be added to the next image acquisition.
The lag is caused by of the thickness of the scintillator and the trapped charges in the a-Si. The
panel will not flush the photodiode instantaneously.
0012_LXVI
Figure 5.71 Ghost image
5.31.4 How to avoid a radar artifact?
There are two methods on how to avoid a radar artifact:
• Reduce the direct exposure of the detector
• Use a compensator or F1 filter.
