5.31.7 Image characteristics that cannot be resolved
5.31.7.1 Radar artifact
You can see radar artifacts in reconstructed VolumeView™ images, usually in the Transverse view
image pane in Figure 5.77.
002685 ©2011 Elekta Limited
Figure 5.77 Example radar artifact
Document ID: 1522605_06 XVI R5.0x
Corrective Maintenance Manual
Page 258 © Elekta Group 2021. All rights reserved.

Troubleshooting
Diagnosing image quality problems
Radar artifacts appear as a circular segment of decreased pixel values around a part of the edge
of the patient. Radar artifacts can be caused when a part of the kV detector panel is directly
exposed to high levels of radiation during a scan. This occurs for example when an image
acquisition laterally through a pelvis is done.
The necessary level of exposure for a thick region of the patient usually causes image saturation
or over-exposure of the kV detector panel where the X-rays go through air. The part of the kV
detector panel, that was exposed to the high levels of radiation shows high sensitivity for some
time.
When the XVI system rotates around the patient, and acquires images through anatomy, the high
sensitivity of the kV detector panel continues to have an effect on the projection images. This can
cause an apparent decrease in the reconstructed density for the region on the opposite side of
the patient to the ‘saturating’ projection angle.
5.31.7.2 Outline reduction effect
The outline reduction effect can occur in reconstructed VolumeView™ images, and causes a
decreased patient outline. Figure 5.78 shows an example of the outline reduction effect, seen
during image registration in green-purple mode. The reconstructed VolumeView™ image (green)
appears smaller than the reference CT volume (purple).
002686 ©2011 Elekta Limited
Figure 5.78 Example of the ‘outline reduction’ effect
Projection rays that go through thinner or less dense areas of the patient anatomy, can cause
panel saturation because of their high transmitted intensity, and this can cause the outline
reduction effect. The reconstruction algorithm in XVI, identifies these saturated pixel values and
treats them as air in subsequent procedures.
5.31.7.3 Correcting the ring artifacts (in axial slices)
In the VolumeView™ windows, ring artifacts appear as one or more rings in the Transverse image
pane, with the center along the axis of rotation (the linear accelerator G–T axis). In the Coronal or
Sagittal image panes, the same artifacts can also appear as lines, as they are cylindrical or conical
XVI R5.0x Document ID: 1522605_06
Corrective Maintenance Manual
© Elekta Group 2021. All rights reserved. Page 259

Troubleshooting
Diagnosing image quality problems
structures. Set patterns or incorrect image content in the projection images that is not related to
the projection angle, usually cause ring artifacts.
The location, radius and severity of the rings is related to the type of the defects in the
projections:
• Clear ‘sharp’ rings – Usually show that there are small high contrast defects, such as
bad pixels, in the kV detector panel. These ring artifacts usually appear in all
reconstructed scans, until the cause of the problem is removed (for example, make a
new bad pixel map).
• One or more low contrast ‘soft’ rings – There is possibly a faint structure in the
acquired images. Residual latency from an earlier high exposure acquisition can cause
this. These artifacts can change with workload. See Figure 5.79.
002687 ©2011 Elekta Limited
Figure 5.79 Example of soft ring artifacts in a CATPhan phantom image
If you get images with almost the same patterns (soft ring artifacts) as shown in Figure 5.79.
1 Try to play the acquired projection set as a movie and look for any structure that does not
vary as the XVI system rotates around the patient.
Related Links:
Correcting images with MFOV ring artifacts on page 281
5.31.7.4 Streak artifacts in axial slices
In the VolumeView™ windows, streak artifacts are lines or bands of artificially lighter or darker
pixels across an image. Streak artifacts can occur if the cone-beam reconstruction procedure is
susceptible to discontinuities, or if there are inconsistencies between adjacent projection images
caused by sources, such as:
Document ID: 1522605_06 XVI R5.0x
Corrective Maintenance Manual
Page 260 © Elekta Group 2021. All rights reserved.

Troubleshooting
Diagnosing image quality problems
• Edges in the image field that are inadequately sampled by the small number of
projections (that is, aliasing effects). Objects in the field-of-view (or included in a
subset of the projection set) that have hard high contrast edges can cause streaks in
the reconstructed slices. The ‘object’ is possibly a part of the patient (for example,
bony edges, teeth, metal inserts) or a part of the equipment (for example, the edge of
the table top or a part of an immobilization device). To find the cause of these artifacts,
try to follow the streaks back to the source, and if necessary, reconstruct a larger
volume.
• Inconsistent beam hardening. As X-ray radiation goes through the patient, its spectral
profile changes as lower energy photons are absorbed (‘beam hardening’). Where
adjacent X-rays go through materials with different atomic numbers (Z), the degree of
the beam hardening of these X-rays can change. This can cause material in the
‘shadow’ of high-Z material to appear to have a different density to material that is the
same, but in a different area, this causes streaking. Figure 5.80 shows an example of
streak artifacts caused by beam hardening from spinal vertebrae.
002688 ©2011 Elekta Limited
Figure 5.80 Example of streak artifacts because of inconsistent beam hardening
• Lag in the kV detector panel. This is a property of a-Si detectors. One sign of lag is that
the pixel response at the start of an exposure takes a few frames until it is at its
nominal value. When you do a 360° image acquisition, the first and last frames of the
projection sequence are acquired through the same patient anatomy, but, because of
lag, the last projection has a higher signal level than the first. This inconsistency can
cause a streak across the image at an orientation that is the same as the start/stop
angle of the scan.
XVI R5.0x Document ID: 1522605_06
Corrective Maintenance Manual
© Elekta Group 2021. All rights reserved. Page 261

Troubleshooting
Diagnosing image quality problems

5.31.8 Correction image symptoms - Rings on images
5.31.8.1 Description of the possible causes for rings to appear in images
Rings appear in images for different reasons:
• Ghost (latency)
• Problems with the badpixel map
• Unwanted material that absorbs radiation on the collimator (lead swarf)
• Problems during calibration with the water tank
• Unwanted material that absorbs radiation on the detector touchguard.
There can be a combination of these effects, and the different problems must be isolated to find a
solution. Some of the problems are system characteristics and can not be resolved.

Troubleshooting
Diagnosing image quality problems
5.31.9 Ghost (latency) artifacts
Ghost (latency)artifacts occur when you can see some remaining signal from a previous
acquisition in the new images that you acquire. Latency is caused because incident radiation has
small temporary effects on the sensitivity of the CsI scintillator in the kV detector panel. This
change in sensitivity can have an effect on subsequent images. The latency effect is temporary
and its magnitude is related to the level of the earlier exposure to the kV detector panel.
The effect that the artifact has on clinical operation is related to the severity of the ghost (latency)
artifact and also the type of ghost artifact.
5.31.9.1 Correcting the ghost (latency) artifacts from a previous PlanarView™ or
MotionView™ acquisition
Ghost (latency) artifacts from a previous PlanarView™ or MotionView™ acquisition are usually
very structured, since the patient was in the same position in relation to the kV detector panel.
Figure 5.104 shows an example of latency from a previous head and neck acquisition, on a new
head and neck image.
002689 ©2011 Elekta Limited
Figure 5.104 Example ghost (latency) artifact from a previous PlanarView™ acquisition
If the image has a pattern as shown in Figure 5.104, follow the procedure as follows:
1 Keep the XVI system switched on as long as possible with this image.
The ghost (latency) artifact will disappear after some time when the image is left alone for a
long time (for example 48 hours).
2 Do the procedure Calibrating multilevel gain images.
XVI R5.0x Document ID: 1522605_06
Corrective Maintenance Manual
© Elekta Group 2021. All rights reserved. Page 289

Troubleshooting
Diagnosing image quality problems
Related Links:
Calibrating multilevel gain images on page 764
5.31.9.2 Ghost (latency) artifacts from previous VolumeView™ acquisitions
Ghost (latency) artifacts from previous VolumeView™ acquisitions are usually not structured (that
is, blurred), because the kV detector panel continuously moves in relation to the patient. A
VolumeView™ of an object with symmetrical edges (for example, phantoms) can cause steep
latency gradients.
If you do a reconstruction on a VolumeView™ image set that has ghost artifacts
It is possible that you will see a type of ring artifact in the reconstructed slices. Refer to section
Correcting the ring artifacts (in axial slices).
If you do consecutive acquisitions with different collimators
Later images can be subtly affected by the collimator choice made in earlier acquisitions.
Figure 5.105 shows an example of a ghost (latency) artifact from a medium field of view
acquisition made earlier, on a new small field of view acquisition.
002690 ©2011 Elekta Limited
Figure 5.105 Example of a ghost (latency) artifact from a collimator used before

5.31.10 Correcting other image problems
5.31.10.1 Correcting a white images (displayed pixel values) fault for the SHF435 kV
generator
The image acquisitions are complete, but no trigger pulses are sent to the kV generator.
1 Open the DCB Utility program.
2 Select the check box Continuous messages.
3 Make sure that there are constant trigger pulses to the generator interface PCB.
4 Make sure that the green LED (D8) flashes at a constant rate.
5 Examine the cables and connectors.
6 Examine the DCB output.
If the kV pulses are missing, the DCB can be defective.
7 If there are trigger pulses at the input to the generator Interface PCB, measure the signal at
the intersection of R13 and Pin 6 of the opto TX U2. Make sure that the waveform looks the
same as the bottom waveform (2) (see Figure 5.106).
This test makes sure that there is an Ext Sync input to the generator (output of the Interface
PCB).

2

Interface PCB
If the pulses do not decrease to less than 2 V, or are missing, the interface PCB can be
defective.

5.31.10.4 Correcting the saturated pixels around the Catphan® image
The effect of saturated pixels around a Catphan® phantom appeared in the reconstruction images
on some XVI systems (see Figure 5.108). It was only reported after extensive image testing or
when higher value mA and mS settings were used than those in the default presets.
005733_001
Figure 5.108 Saturated pixels around a Catphan® image

2 If the problem continues, let the panel stay for 12 hours without taking scans and then do
the procedure Single level and Filter calibration and if required Calibrating multilevel gain
images.
3 If possible, reduce the mA and ms for the scan.
4 Make sure that the Hounsfield calibration values are correct.
5 Set the reconstruction preset parameter ReconstructionDataType=integer for MFOV or
LFOV only.
Note: Depending on your hardware configuration, you can experience a longer reconstruction time.

5.31.10.5 Correcting the saturation artifacts in reconstructed images
With the introduction of Hounsfield Unit scaling in XVI R5.0, saturation of projection images can
cause unwanted artifacts to appear in an image. The white streaks (see Figure 5.109) are caused
by saturated pixels.
005371 ©2013 Elekta Limited
Figure 5.109 Saturation artifact
1 If this problem appears, make sure that the values in the gain preset are the default values.
2 If the problem continues, let the panel stay for 12 hours without taking scans and then do
the procedure Single level and Filter calibration and if necessary Calibrating multilevel gain
images.
3 If possible, reduce the mA and ms for the scan.
4 Set the value of air to 1 to remove the saturation artifact if you do not want to use
Hounsfield Unit scaling.
5 Set the reconstruction preset parameter ReconstructionDataType=integer for MFOV or
LFOV only.
Your hardware configuration determines the time that is necessary to complete
reconstruction.
Note: Do not use an integer for a scan which is less than 350 degrees.
Note: If you want to use Hounsfield Unit scaling, see the XVI Imaging White Paper which is available on
the Elekta Marketing website.

5.31.11.3 Warming up the X-ray tube
The kV Tube Warmup inhibit appears if there is a time delay of four hours since the last image
acquisition.
Note: While the X-ray tube warms up, images appear in the image pane in the XVI window, but these
are not saved in the patient database.
Document ID: 1522605_06 XVI R5.0x
Corrective Maintenance Manual
Page 314 © Elekta Group 2021. All rights reserved.

Troubleshooting
Correcting Intrafraction Imaging problems
XVI uses a three step procedure to warm up the X-ray tube. Each step changes the kV, mA, and ms
settings, which helps to prevent arcing of the X-ray tube. The arcing can cause an image
acquisition to terminate.
Note: You can warm up the X-ray tube at all angles, with all collimator cassettes and filter cassettes, but
it is good procedure to use the smallest collimator cassette.
1 Extend the kV source arm.
CAUTION 5.3
Do not warm up the X-ray tube if the kV detector panel is open. If the kV detector panel is not
retracted, you will possibly irradiate the detector panel electronics. If you ignore this caution,
you can cause damage to the equipment.
2 Retract the kV detector panel.
3 Make sure that there are no inhibits.
4 On the menu bar, click Image > kV Acquisition > Tube Warmup.
Note: All the other acquisition types are unavailable at this time, and appear dimmed.
The Tube Warm-up dialog box appears.
5 On the FKP, press the kV START button.
Note: If you press the kV INTERRUPT button before the X-ray tube warm up is completed, then you
cannot continue with that warm up beam. The subsequent warm up beam will start.
The radiation warning lamp comes on, the kV generator status changes to X-ray, and the
Exposing dialog box appears. XVI shows the frame count on the status bar and on the frame
counter in the image tool panel. The heat units on the status bar update to show the
capacity of the X-ray tube. When the beam is completed, the Tube Warm-up dialog box
appears with the message Ready to warm-up the tube, and shows the current
warm up step.
6 On the FKP, press the kV START button.
When the beam is completed, the Tube Warm-up dialog box appears with the message
Ready to warm-up the tube, and shows the current warm up step.
7 On the FKP, press the kV START button.
XVI automatically does a kV offset calibration, and the kV Offset Calibration inhibit shows in
the inhibit status indicator.

5.32.4 Disabling Pulseline artifact correction
To check if the pulseline artifact is working, disable the correction and check if image quality is
degraded. Do the same for MV scatter correction. Image quality will be degraded if MV scatter
correction is off.
1 Disable pulseline correction by removing the parameter
[Imaging_KV]MVBandKernel1= in the sri.ini file.
By default, pulseline artifact correction is on when [Imaging_KV]MVBandKernel1=3.
5.32.5 Confirming MV scatter correction is working
1 Set the DisableMVScatterCorrection flag in sri.ini[Imaging_KV] to 0.
MVScatterCorrection is turned off.

5.33 Correcting the Reconstruction does not show the correct
Hounsfield Unit value
1 Make sure the system is calibrated for the preset you are using.
2 Do a check the message log to see what Air and Water values were used, if they are 0 then
Hounsfield Unit scaling was not applied.
3 In the sri.ini file, make sure that ProjectionImageScaling =1.

5.35 Doing the alternative calibration procedure to improve
image quality
This procedure can improve the image quality of the XVI system, particularly for contrast
resolution of soft tissue. The kV source arm and detector panel is accurately aligned with the
lasers and a larger range of gain images are used.
This procedure requires the digital accelerator cross wires to be set within 0.5 mm at isocenter
and 1 mm at the floor. The lasers are required to be adjusted to within 0.5 mm in all axes up to 1
m from the isocenter, so additional time should be allowed for this alignment. Depending on
experience and type of lasers installed this additional setup can take up to an additional 4 hours.
If the hospital will not allow the adjustment of the lasers (particularly during maintenance)
because of local procedures, then the gain calibration method can still be used without the
additional accurate alignment of the X-ray tube and kV detector panel.
1 Align the lasers.
XVI R5.0x Document ID: 1522605_06
Corrective Maintenance Manual
© Elekta Group 2021. All rights reserved. Page 319

Doing the alternative calibration procedure to improve image quality
The lateral and sagittal lasers in the treatment room must follow the X, Y and Z axes of XVI
from the isocenter, with a maximum deviation of ± 0.5 mm for at least 1 meter from the
isocenter in all directions.
The laser lines must be vertical when measured using a plumbline with a maximum
deviation of ± 0.5 mm for at least 1 meter from the isocenter.
a Examine the crosswires (see Examining the crosswires).
b Install the A side laser (see Installing the A side laser).
c Install the B side laser (see Installing the B side laser).
d Find the mechanical isocenter (see Finding the mechanical isocenter).

check to make sure that the laser is at the mechanical isoceneter).
f Align the sagittal laser (see Aligning the sagittal laser).
2 Align the X-ray tube (see Aligning the X-ray tube).
The central axis of the X-ray beam shall follow the central axis of the Megavoltage beam
within 0.5 mm.
3 Align the kV detector panel (seeCalibrating the kV detector arm (hardware)).
4 Do a single level gain and filter calibration or a multilevel gain calibration.
5 Acquire kV flexmaps (see Acquiring the kV flexmaps).

5.35.1 Examining the crosswires
1 Attach a piece of paper (graph paper if available) on the treatment table at the isocenter.
2 Rotate the gantry and BLD to 0°.
3 Put a mark of the crosswires on the paper.
4 Rotate the BLD to 0, 90, 180 and 270°.
5 Confirm that the crosswires are centered with a deviation of a maximum of 0.5 mm.
6 Rotate the table 90°, use the column rotation.
7 Attach a piece of paper (graph paper if available) to the floor and mark the crosswires.
8 Rotate the BLD to 0, 90, 180 and 270°.
9 Confirm that the crosswires are centered with a deviation of a maximum of 1 mm.
10 If the crosswires are not within these limits at isocenter and the floor, adjust them until
they are set correctly.

5.35.2 Installing the A side laser
1 Use the the column rotation to rotate the table to 0°.
2 Rotate the gantry to +88° and mark the center of the crosswire on the wall.
3 Rotate the gantry to +92° and mark the center of the crosswire on the wall.
4 Join these marks with a line.
5 Rotate the gantry to 90° ± 0.2 mm/m, use a spirit level on the head ring.
6 Mark the center of where the crosswire projects onto the wall.
7 Install the laser so that:
– The horizontal laser is positioned on the center mark.
– The vertical laser positioned on the vertical line.
– Adjust the lasers so they are aligned to the cross wires to a maximum of 0.5 mm over
a 1m range from isocenter and within 1 mm across the length of room.

5.35.4 Finding the mechanical isocenter
1 Rotate the gantry and BLD to 0°.
2 Attach a mechanical pointer (for example a 1.5 mm ball end hex key fixed to the table)
approximately at isocenter.
3 Center the ball end of the hex key on the cross wire.
4 Rotate the gantry to +90°.
5 Adjust the table height so that the ball is centered on the cross wire.
6 Rotate the gantry to 0° and confirm the ball end is still on the cross wire.
7 Rotate the gantry to 180°.
8 Measure the error in the GT direction through the 180° rotation and position the
mechanical pointer so that it is positioned in the center of the movement (halve the error).
9 Measure the error in the AB direction through the 180° rotation and position the
mechanical pointer so that it is positioned in the center of the movement (halve the error).
10 Rotate the gantry from +180 to -180 and confirm that the deviation of the mechanical
pointer is less than 1 mm from the cross wires in any direction.
The ball end is now at the mechanical isocenter.

5.35.5 Doing a check to make sure that the laser is at the mechanical
isocenter
1 Make sure that the lateral A side horizontal and vertical lasers are within 0.5 mm of the
mechanical pointer. Adjust if required.
2 Make sure that the lateral B side horizontal and vertical lasers are within 0.5 mm of the
mechanical pointer. Adjust if required.
3 Make sure that the A and B side lateral lasers are within ± 1 mm of each other across the
entire room. Adjust if required.
4 Use a plumbline, make sure that the lateral A side vertical laser has a maximum deviation of
± 0.5 mm for a minimum of 1 m from the isocenter. Adjust if required.
5 Use a plumbline, make sure that the lateral B side vertical laser has a maximum deviation of
± 0.5 mm for a minimum of 1 m from the isocenter. Adjust if required.

5.35.6 Aligning the sagittal laser
1 Establish the rotational center of the gantry.
2 Attach a piece of paper to the front gantry cover, project a shadow on to the cover
approximately 10 cm from the center using an external light source.
3 Mark on the paper the position of this shadow for the cardinal angles 0, 90, 180 and 270°
gantry rotation.
4 Join the marks with a ruler for 0 and 180 and 90 and 270.
The intersection is the mechanical center of rotation.
5 Take a fine string from the rotational center of the gantry through the center of the ball
(mechanical isocenter) on to the sagittal wall.
6 Mark the position of this string on the sagittal wall.
7 Align the sagittal laser to the alignment string to within +/- 0.5 mm in the range 1 m from
isocenter.
8 Hang a plumbline and confirm that the sagittal laser is within +/- 0.5 mm of the plumb line
in the range 1 m from the isocenter.
The lasers are now setup accurately enough to be used when checking or aligning the X-ray
tube and Imaging Panel.

5.35.7 Aligning the X-ray tube
Prerequisites
The central axis of the X-ray beam shall follow the central axis of the Megavoltage beam within
0.5 mm.
1 Press the button and extend the X-ray tube and release the button to lock in position.
2 Remove the cover from the gantry side of the tube housing.
3 Rotate the gantry to -90° and place a level in the GT direction on the aluminum collimator
housing.
4 If the collimator is not level, remove the source arm covers.
5 Do the procedure Adjusting the level of the X-ray tube and collimator assembly horizontally.
6 Put the CAL1 filter into the collimator.
7 Rotate the machine to approximately 20° and observe the lateral vertical laser as it
impinges on the tube housing. Make small changes to the gantry angle until the laser is as
close as possible to the "Anode line" on the tube housing. Adjust the extension of the tube
arm until the laser is pointing to the anode line ± 1 mm.
8 Rotate the gantry to 0° confirm that the vertical laser is centered on the cross at the center
of CAL1.
9 Make small adjustments to the tube extension position and tube angle until these two
conditions are satisfied ± 1 mm.

Troubleshooting
Correcting the bad pixel map manually
It is possible that the bad pixel map calibration wizard incorrectly identifies pixels as bad when
the default settings are used.
A custom setting can be used to update the badpixel map manually.
1 Log on to the service database as a service2 user.
2 Create a new patient with the name Manual BadPixel.
3 Create a treatment with an ID equal to the current date.
4 In the Field pane, select a kV Image.

5.37 Description of the multilevel gain files
Five different multigain-images (MLG) files are created in C:\XVI\Calmig\Current\kV\gain. Each
file has a different number at the end of the file name. The files with the ending .bak are backup
files. See examples in Figure 5.148.

5.41 Correcting a failure to access a shared folder
When you try to open a shared folder on a different server or drive, the message that follows
appears: You can't access this shared folder because your
organization's security policies block unauthenticated guest
access. These policies help protect your PC from unsafe or
malicious devices on the network.
1 If the above error message appears, click Control Panel > User Accounts > Credential
Manager > Manage Windows Credentials .
2 Click Add a generic credential.
3 In the Internet or network address box, type the server or drive address.

Service Parts Repair or Replacement
Important information about service work on the XVI system
6.1 Important information about service work on the XVI
system
Note: Make sure that you replace fuses with the same type and rating or use an Elekta replacement
part.
Radiation warning lamp
WARNING 6.1
Ionizing radiation is hazardous to health. Do not go into the treatment room while the
RADIATION warning lamp near the treatment room door is on (illuminated). If you ignore this
warning, it can cause fatal injury.
Copying calibration factors to XVI after table or gantry recalibration
The table or gantry movements can be re-calibrated after work on the system, for example after a
potentiometer was changed. It is very important to then run the XVI DCB Utility and copy the
table and gantry calibration factors to the XVI system. These calibration factors are not copied to
XVI automatically. If this is not done then there could be a mismatch between where the table
actually is and where XVI thinks the table is, hence when the XVI calculates the table corrections
they may not be correct.
WARNING 6.2
If the gantry movements are re-calibrated, run the XVI DCB Utility and copy the gantry
calibration factors to XVI. If you ignore this warning, you can cause clinical mistreatment.
Overheating of the X-ray tube
CAUTION 6.1
The X-ray tube can be damaged if it is used at continuous high power levels for long periods.
The kV generator monitors the temperature of the kV source and prohibits radiation if the
temperature exceeds approximately 85°C. However, the heat capacity of the kV housing is greater
than that of the anode of the X-ray tube. Consequently the anode may overheat before the
maximum temperature of the kV source reaches 85°C.
Information for set up, test and calibration
WARNING 6.3
Replacing parts can have an effect on the calibration of the XVI system. Record all parts
replaced during the troubleshooting procedure. After the repair, do all test, set up and
calibration procedures that are given in the replacement procedures for those parts. If you
ignore this warning, you can cause clinical mistreatment.
Information for the administrator
If you are using XVI R5.0.6 with Mk6i control cabinet, the username of the administrator is elekta.

Doing servicing activities with the kV source arm extended
For software releases before R4.5 the foot switch is used instead of the function keypad (FKP) and
the gantry rotation is controlled by the treatment control cabinet (TCC).
Tools, material, and test equipment
• Standard tool kit for Elekta engineers
• Special tools, test equipment, or material from the list in the applicable procedures
(where necessary).
6.2 Doing servicing activities with the kV source arm extended
WARNING 6.4
Do not do work on the equipment before you disconnect the electrical supply to the work area
to prevent a high voltage electric shock. If you ignore this warning, you can cause fatal injury.
WARNING 6.5
Do not work in the treatment or equipment room in the path of the kV source arms. If you
ignore this cautions, you can cause serious injury and damage to the equipment.
1 Extend the kV source arm to its farthest position and attach a cable tie around the lock
mechanism of the kV source arm.
2 Put a warning note on the lock switch with the message that follows: Servicing
activity in progress. Do not retract the kV source arm!
3 When the service is completed, remove the cable tie from the lock mechanism of the kV
source arm.
4 Remove the warning note from the lock switch.
5 Retract the kV source arm.
WARNING 6.6
Do not extend or retract the kV source arm, or the shafts, if persons are in the path of
movement behind the gantry. If you ignore this warning, you can cause serious injury.
6 Make sure that the kV source arm interlock operates correctly.

2 Extend the kV source arm to the imaging position.
3 Remove the three screws (1) and remove the touchguard (see Figure 6.1).

4 Do a VolumeView™ scan and make sure that no errors appear on the acquired image.
a Look for ring artifacts that were not on previous acquired images.
b If there are ring artifacts, examine the collimator cassette or filter cassette for:
‒ Damage
‒ Unwanted material on the cassette or filter cassette
‒ See the Troubleshooting section for more information.

6.7.5 Removing the long roller blind assembly
1 At the interface cabinet or RIC, switch off the circuit breaker CB14 (iView arm).
2 Remove the detector touchguard.
3 Choose your action based on your machine type.
If Then
Your machine is not Harmony. Go to step 4 .
Your machine is Harmony. Remove the scroll cover on the front cover.

blind. You will find these below the blind.

6.7.6 Installing the long roller blind assembly
1 Install the roller blind assembly in position.
2 Install the two fixing nuts, and then two (non-Harmony) or three (Harmony) fixing screws at
the gantry end.
3 Choose your action based on your machine type.
If Then
Your machine is not Harmony. Go to step 4 .
Your machine is Harmony. Install the scroll cover on the front cover.
4 At the interface cabinet, switch on the circuit breaker CB14 (iView arm).
5 Make sure that the roller blind operates correctly along its full range of movement.
6 Install the detector touchguard.

6.7.12 Installing the lateral drive assembly
1 Install the new lateral drive assembly into position.
2 Loosely install the fixing screws through the cradle into the gearbox housing assembly and
into the center nut housing assembly.
3 Move the cradle to the end of its travel in the B-direction.
4 Tighten the center nut housing assembly and gearbox housing assembly.
5 Before you assemble the lateral drive again, do these steps:
a Operate the lateral drive from the HHC and make sure that the microswitches are
operated at each of the end stops.
b Make sure that there are no tight areas.
A tight area can cause the DC motor to decrease in speed or sound as if the motor is
put in unwanted load.
c If the sound or the speed of the motor decreases, do steps d to g .
d Stop the motor and release the screws of the center nut housing assembly.
e Turn the center nut housing assembly a small distance.
This will remove the tight area.
f Tighten the center nut housing assembly and examine the full travel again.
g Do d to f again when tight areas prevent the full range of lateral movement.
6 Connect the two lateral potentiometer wires to the lateral drive assembly.
7 Make sure that the lateral drive operates correctly along the full range of lateral movement
and that it is clear of all cables.
8 Use Loctite® 222 for the two.
9 Install the kV detector panel and detector touchguard.
10 Make sure that the lateral drive system operates correctly at the gantry angles 0°, 90°, 180°
and 270°.
11 Do the procedure Calibrating the kV detector arm (software).
12 Do the procedure Doing a test of the 3D image quality.
13 Do the procedure Doing a test of the 3D Registration accuracy.

potentiometer cable
A new connector is now used on the detector touchguard and lateral motor cables
(4513 341 2203), and the lateral potentiometer cable (4513 341 1846). This new connector is
slightly larger than the old one and the feeding of the cable through the cradle tube is very
difficult. Therefore, you must disassemble the connector before you feed the cable through the
cradle tube.
This section describes how to disassemble the connector. For the installation of the cables, refer
to the latest iViewGT™ Corrective Maintenance Manual for the procedure to replace these cables.

6.8.10 Adjusting the position of the brake assembly
1 Turn the adjustment bolt until the two belts have the correct tension.
2 Loosen the screws on the clutch assembly, and at the same time, make sure that the central
axis of the clutch assembly is vertical.
3 When the two belts are at equal tension, tighten the screws. Use Loctite® 222 on the fixing
screws.
4 Move the mid-point of the side of the belt without a clamp (B-side of top belt and A-side of
lower belt) by a distance of 10 mm.
5 Measure the necessary force to get step 4 with an applicable spring balance.
6 Adjust the brake unit adjustment bolt until you get a force of approximately 7.5 N (750 g).
See Figure 6.101.

6.11.2 Adding silicone oil to the HT tank connector sockets
Prerequisites
• Lint-free cloths
• 500 ml silicone oil (1501453)
• Small syringe (hospital supplied)
1 Switch off the generator and isolate the electrical supply at the three-phase circuit breaker.
2 Let the HT capacitors discharge for a minimum of 3 minutes.
3 Use a steel rule, and put it down the center of one of the HT connector sockets until it
touches the bottom of the socket.
4 Measure the level of the silicone oil.
5 Fill the syringe with the silicone oil.
6 Carefully add the silicone oil to one of the HT connector sockets until the oil level is
approximately at the 1 cm mark (see Figure 6.127).

6.12.3 Adding silicone oil to the HT tank connector sockets
Prerequisites
• Lint-free cloths
• 500 ml silicone oil (1501453)
• Small syringe (hospital supplied)
1 Switch off the generator and isolate the electrical supply at the three-phase circuit breaker.
2 Let the HT capacitors discharge for a minimum of 3 minutes.
3 Use a steel rule, and put it down the center of one of the HT connector sockets until it
touches the bottom of the socket.
4 Measure the level of the silicone oil.
5 Fill the syringe with the silicone oil.
6 Carefully add the silicone oil to one of the HT connector sockets until the oil level is
approximately at the 1 cm mark (see Figure 6.132).

6.13.3 Repair and replacement of the PSU of the kV detector arm
6.13.3.1 Removing the detector arm PSU
1 Switch off the digital accelerator, XVI, and iViewGT™ systems.
2 Disconnect the mains cable, the detector power cable and the sense cable and cut cable
ties as required to remove the PSU tray assembly from the gantry.
3 Remove the PSU tray assembly from the gantry.
4 Remove the defective PSU from the PSU tray assembly.
5 Loosen the four screws on the bottom of the assembly.
6 Disconnect all external connections to the defective PSU.
Related Links:
Installing the detector arm PSU on page 493
6.13.3.2 Installing the detector arm PSU
1 Connect the applicable cables to the new PSU.
2 Install the new PSU to the PSU tray assembly.
3 Install the PSU tray assembly to the gantry.
CAUTION 6.17
Do not switch on the digital accelerator, XVI, and iViewGT™ systems unless the power and
sense cables are installed correctly. If you ignore this caution, you can cause damage to the
equipment.
4 Connect the mains cable, the detector power cable and the sense cable.
5 Switch on the digital accelerator, XVI, and iViewGT™ systems.
6 Look for LED activity on the ACB PCB.
7 Examine the -24 V and +24 V on TP1 and TP2 and on the ACB PCB.
8 Make sure that all detector arm movements operate correctly.
6.13.4 Repair and replacement of the PSU of the kV detector panel
6.13.4.1 Removing the detector panel PSU
1 Switch off the digital accelerator, XVI, and iViewGT™ systems.
2 Disconnect all cables from the PSU tray assembly.
3 Remove the PSU tray assembly from the gantry.
4 Remove the defective PSU from the PSU assembly.
5 Loosen the four screws located on the bottom of the assembly.
6 Disconnect all external connections to the defective PSU.

6.15 XVI control cabinet
6.15.1 Accessing the XVI control cabinet
1 Make sure the power to the XVI control cabinet is switched off.
WARNING 6.37
If the XVI control cabinet is energized from a UPS, remove the power from the XVI control
cabinet with the switch on the UPS output and remove the cables between the UPS and the XVI
control cabinet. The UPS can supply power after the mains supply is removed from its input. If
you ignore this warning, it can cause fatal injury.
Document ID: 1522605_06 XVI R5.0x
Corrective Maintenance Manual
Page 518 © Elekta Group 2021. All rights reserved.

Service Parts Repair or Replacement
XVI control cabinet
2 If the XVI control cabinet is energized from a UPS, switch off the UPS output.
WARNING 6.38
Make sure that electrical power is removed from the XVI control cabinet before you open the
side panels. If you ignore this warning, you can get an electric shock. The electric shock can
cause fatal injury.
WARNING 6.39
Before you do servicing or maintenance work on the digital accelerator, use the standby screen
to find if a restart is scheduled. Find the date and the time of the next restart.Make sure that
the equipment can not start suddenly while you work on it. If you ignore this warning, this can
cause fatal injury.
WARNING 6.40
If the digital accelerator is configured to stay in an initialized status when outside working
hours, then the interface cabinet will contain energized parts. If you ignore this warning, this
can cause fatal injury.
CAUTION 6.19
Do not touch the printed circuit boards (PCBs) and other electronic parts unless you wear a
grounded antistatic wrist strap. If you ignore this caution, electrostatic discharge can cause
damage to the parts and can be the cause of a malfunction.
3 At the back of the XVI control cabinet, remove the three screws in each of the side panels
and remove the two side panels.
6.15.2 Replacing the hard disk drive
CAUTION 6.20
If the hard disk drive operates, back up the files before you replace it. There is an Elekta option
(XML Archive) to help with the back up of drive D. Otherwise the contents of the hard disk drive
will be lost.
CAUTION 6.21
Do not restore the system to an earlier software release after a hard disk drive failure. Earlier
software releases do not have the latest safety items installed.
1 Make sure that what follows is available before you start:
– The XVI Backup and Restore CD ROM
– The most recent back up files for the drive
– The two SATA hard disks configured as drive D (RAID 0)
– The CD/DVD drive.
These are necessary for the replacement of the recovery DVDs.
The IDE hard disk configured as drive C.

6.15.4 Partitioning and restoring drive C
1 Tell the system administrator of the hospital to make a backup of the Drive D.
2 When the drive D backup is completed, find the Snapshot, backup and restore CD and the
last XVI Recovery DVD set.
3 Put the Snapshot, backup and restore CD in the CD/DVD drive of the XVI control cabinet and
start the Windows® operating system again.
4 When a message appears, press a key to start the XVI control cabinet from the CD.
The command prompt appears.
5 At the command prompt, type diskpart and press ENTER.
6 Type list volume and press ENTER.
This shows the volumes on the XVI control cabinet.
7 Make sure that the drive letter (Ltr) for the CD/DVD drive is set to E.
8 If it is not set to E, type select volume and the volume number for CD/DVD drive, and
then press ENTER.
9 Type assign letter=E and press ENTER.
10 Type exit and press ENTER.
11 Type E:\snapshot and press ENTER.
The Snapshot - Startup Screen dialog box appears.

This section shows how to make disk checks and defragmentation scripts/batch files and how to
set them to run automatically. Elekta recommends to wait for a minimum of 30 minutes after a
script file is run before you run one more. A description of the script filenames is given in Table
6.6.
Table 6.6 Script filename descriptions
Filename Description Interval
XVI_Disk_Defrag_D.bat Defragments drive D on the XVI control cabinet Daily
and logs the results in: C:\XVI\Logs|DiskAnalysis
\DiskDefrag.log
XVI_Disk_Analysis.bat Analyses drive D on the XVI control cabinet and Daily
logs the results in: C:\XVI\Logs\DiskAnalysis
\DiskAnalysis.log
XVI_Disk_Fix_D.bat Scans drive D on the XVI control cabinet for bad Daily
sectors, it tries to fix any that have been found
and logs the results in: C:\XVI\Logs\DiskAnalysis
\DiskFix.log
6.15.15.1 Writing the defragmentation batch
1 Log on as Administrator at the XVI control cabinet.
2 Go to the directory C:\XVI and make a new text file with the filename
XVI_Disk_Defrag_D.txt.
3 In this new text file type the text that follows:
@echo off
REM ** Defrag the D: drive
REM ** Note this script will be run from the windows schedule
REM ** Set the logging directory
set LOGDIR=C:\XVI\Logs\DiskAnalysis
if not exist %LOGDIR% mkdir %LOGDIR%
REM ** Set the full path to the file
set LOGFILENAME="%LOGDIR%\DiskDefrag.log"
echo Running XVI Defrag D: to %LOGFILENAME% ...
echo. >> %LOGFILENAME%
REM ** Write Log Header to log file
echo ################################################## >>
%LOGFILENAME%
echo XVI Disk Defrag Started on %DATE% %TIME% >> %LOGFILENAME%
echo ################################################## >>
%LOGFILENAME%
echo. >> %LOGFILENAME%
REM ** Must be run as Administrator
if /i NOT "%USERNAME%"=="administrator" goto not_admin
REM ** Check if XVI is running.
tasklist /fi "imagename eq sri.exe" 2<&1 | findstr /i
"sri.exe" >nul
if %ERRORLEVEL% EQU 0 goto xvi_exists
REM ** Perform the defrag and write to log file, this will
probally take
REM ** some time
REM ** Note defrag is more effective when there is at least
10% free space

XVI control cabinet
echo DEFRAG is running, press <CTRL> + C to cancel
defrag D: >> %LOGFILENAME% 2<&1
echo. >> %LOGFILENAME%
echo Logging completed at %DATE% %TIME% >> %LOGFILENAME%
echo. >> %LOGFILENAME%
goto :eof
:xvi_exists
echo.
echo ERROR: XVI is running.
echo ERROR: XVI is running. >> %LOGFILENAME%
echo.
goto failed_to_finish
:not_admin
echo.
echo This script must be run as Administrator.
echo This script must be run as Administrator. >> %LOGFILENAME
%
echo.
goto failed_to_finish
:failed_to_finish
echo XVI Disk Defrag D: failed at %DATE% %TIME%. Exiting ...
echo XVI Disk Defrag D: failed at %DATE% %TIME%. >>
%LOGFILENAME%
echo.
pause

6.15.15.4 Setting up scheduled disk maintenance tasks to run automatically
1 Make sure, that the XVI control cabinet is switched on.
This is necessary for scheduled tasks to run outside of usual clinical hours.
2 Put the password for the user account on which the scheduled task will operate into the
password field.
There must be a password in the password field.
3 Make sure that the date and time on the XVI control cabinet is set correctly.
The Scheduled Tasks program uses the set date and time to operate correctly.
4 Click Start >Programs >Accessories >System Tools > Scheduled Tasks.
5 Double-click Add Scheduled Task.
The Scheduled Task Wizard dialog box appears.
6 Click Next.
7 Click Browse and go to the directory C:\XVI.
8 Select the filename XVI_Disk_Analysis.bat and click Open.
The next window of the Scheduled Task Wizard appears.
9 Select Daily and then click Next.
10 Select the necessary time and day to start the task.
For example; 23:00, Weekdays, 22/06/2010.
11 Then click Next.
12 Type the username and password of the administrator.
13 Select the check box adjacent to; Open advanced properties for this task when I click
Finish
14 Click Finish.
15 Select the Settings tab.
16 Select the check box adjacent to; Stop the task if it runs for
a Set the time to 5 hours.

17 Setting up the XVI Disk Defrag task.
a Do steps 4 to 12.
b At step 7 select the filename XVI_Disk_Defrag_D.bat.
18 Setting up the XVI Disk Fix task.
a Do steps 4 to 12.
b At step 7 select the filename XVI_Disk_Fix_D.bat.
6.15.16 Backing up the database
Elekta recommends that the System Administrator makes sure that backups are made each day of
the XVI database and XVI calibration and system files. By default, their location is D:\ and C:\XVI\.
Use the Windows® task scheduler to schedule these backups. (For information of how to
configure the task schedule, refer to Windows® Help).

You can connect XVI to the hospital LAN, and database backups kept on a remote network
location. The time to complete a backup is related to the size of the database and the transfer
speed on the LAN connection.
WARNING 6.42
Do not use the XVI system clinically during a restore of the XVI patient database from a backup.
If you do, it can cause clinical mistreatment.
WARNING 6.43
If you restore the database from a backup, the System Administrator must make sure that the
correct backup is used. If an incorrect backup is used, it can cause clinical mistreatment.

6.15.19 Recovery of calibration and configuration data from XVI R5.0.2 on
Mk4/Mk5 to XVI R5.0.6 on Mk6i control cabinet
6.15.19.1 Prerequisites to recover data from an XVI R5.0.2 system to an XVI R5.0.6 system
The necessary prerequisites for the replacement of an XVI control cabinet are:
• An image backup on the NSS (or NAS) of the XVI R5.0.2
• A valid XVI R5.0.6 license key
• A hospital Administrator to restore the XVI R5.0.2 databases on to the replacement
Mk6i control cabinet.
Note: It is necessary that you follow all the procedures in this workflow to recover the data from XVI
R5.0.2 system to an XVI R5.0.6 system.

6.15.24 Environmental conditions for the XVI control cabinet
The XVI control cabinet has an internal operating temperature between 0°C to 60°C. The internal
parts give off a large quantity of heat and if this heat is not removed from the system the internal
temperature can increase.
To prevent the XVI control cabinet from getting too hot, make sure that the cooling fan inlets are
clear of any blockages (rear and front fans) and that filters are kept clean. If the control cabinet is
kept in a closed space, sufficient cooling must be given.
Note: If the internal temperature of the XVI control cabinet rises above 60°C the XVI control cabinet will
give an audible indication, also the LED on the front panel labeled Temp will flash.
The switching on of the cooling fans and the temperature threshold - therefore the audible
alarms - are controlled by the DIP switches on the internal temperature monitoring board. These
DIP switches can be examined to make sure that they are set correctly.
Note: The internal temperature monitoring board is located on the base of the inside of the XVI control
cabinet

6.17.1.6 Calibrating the longitudinal movement of the kV detector panel
1 Rotate the gantry to 0° ±0.1°.

This sets the system to LED Extra mode.

the LONG mark.
Note: The Fine A and Fine B potentiometers are approximately 20° out of phase with each other. The
wiper voltages will show this.
4 Measure the voltage on test point TP33 (see Figure 6.349). This is the selected wiper
voltage of the longitudinal fine potentiometer.

6.17.2 Calibrating the kV detector arm (software)
The kV detector arm imaging position calibration uses the XVI program to find the longitudinal
and lateral position of the kV detector panel for the three fields of view (FOV). Use the XVI
handheld controller (HHC) to do the calibration.
1 On the XVI system, log on to the service database as a service2 User.
2 On XVI, select Service > kV Service Functions > kV Panel Position Calibration.
The XVI Calibration window opens.
3 Go into the treatment room.
4 If it is necessary, on the XVI HHC (or Linac HHC), press the MONITOR SWITCH button.
The treatment room monitors (TRM) shows the XVI Calibration window (see Figure 6.353).

Note: The workflow will start when the system senses movement.
5 Retract the kV detector arm to the closed position.
6 Rotate the gantry to -90° ±1°.
7 On the HHC, press and hold the ENABLE bars to move the kV detector arm to the isocenter.
8 Release the ENABLE bars when the kV detector arm is in the correct position.
9 Move the kV detector arm to the small FOV.
When the kV detector arm stops, the yellow radio button on the XVI Calibration window
flashes green for approximately 10 seconds. The radio button indicator goes green and
shows a check mark. This shows that the software has accepted the calibration at this
position.
10 Use the medium FOV button to move the kV detector arm to the medium FOV.
After 10 seconds the medium FOV radio button goes green and shows a check mark.
11 Move the kV detector arm to the large FOV.
After 10 seconds the large FOV radio button indicator goes green and shows a check mark.
12 Move the kV detector arm to the medium FOV from the large FOV.
After 10 seconds the fourth indicator goes green and shows a check mark.
Note: The Save results button is active when steps 8 to 10 are successfully completed. When this
button is clicked, the software saves the values in the sri.ini file.
13 Go into the control room.
14 On the kV Panel Position Calibration window, click Save results.
The Calibration save radio button (5) (see Figure 6.353) goes green and shows a check
mark.

6.17.3.1 Setting up the kV generator
This section gives instructions to set up the kV generator for the use with the XVI system.
WARNING 6.48
The kV generator is connected to the three-phase supply. Do not start the work on the
generator with the isolator switched on. If you ignore this warning, it can cause fatal injury to
you and other persons.
1 Set the kV generator switches. Do the steps that follow:
a Set the ATP controller DIP switches.
b Set the HT controller DIP switch.
c Set the LV DRAC DIP switches.
d Switch on the kV generator.
2 Calibrate the tube filament.
3 Do the steps that follow:
a Switch off the mA closed loop control.
b Calibrate the small focus.
c Calibrate the large focus.
d Complete the generator setup.

the HHC.

When you operate the kV START button in the Sedecal software, the kV Start signal stays.
1 On the FKP, press the kV INTERRUPT button. The radiation cannot be stopped in the Sedecal
software.

This procedure is automated by the kV generator and selects each mA setting used by the
selected focal spot for different kV settings. The generator then increases the filament

6.17.4.4 Calibrating the tube filaments (SHFR 400)
This task is a part of the installation procedure. You must do this task again if you change the X-ray
tube or the kV generator. When you calibrate the X-ray tube filament current, the kV generator
records the parameter values which operates the correct filament currents for kV/mA
combinations.
You must first calibrate the small focal spot filament, then the large focal spot filament.

6.17.5.13 Calibrating the A-B position of the kV detector arm in the small FOV
1 Extend the kV source arm to the imaging position.
2 Put the S20 and CAL1 cassettes into the collimator assembly.
3 Use the HHC to move the kV detector panel to the small FOV.
4 Make sure that the gantry is at –90°.
5 Select the preset Panel Alignment - Small FOV, and acquire a PlanarView™ image at the
small FOV.
6 In the right image area of the XVI window, zoom in to the cross in the center of the small
FOV image.
7 Put the mouse pointer on the vertical line of the cross (G-T axis).
8 Make sure that the pointer is horizontally in the center, and on the line as accurate as
possible.
9 Record the x coordinate value (x,y) in the bottom left corner of the XVI display.
10 Make sure that the coordinate value is 512 pixels ±10 pixels.
11 If the coordinate value is satisfactory, calibrate the A-B position of the kV detector arm in
the large FOV.
12 If the coordinate value is incorrect, do the steps that follow.
a On the ACB, set jumper PL7 to the left position.
The system goes to LED Extra mode.
b Move the kV detector arm until the sagittal laser aligns with the S mark.
c If the kV detector arm stops before this position, on the ACB, set jumper PL9 to the
left position.
The system goes to override lateral limit.
d Adjust potentiometer RV4 until LED 3.7 comes on.
e On the ACB, set jumper PL7 to the right position.
The system goes to LED Normal mode.
f Set jumper PL9 to the right position.
Related Links:
Calibrating the A-B position of the kV detector arm in the large FOV on page 695
6.17.5.14 Calibrating the A-B position of the kV detector arm in the large FOV
1 Replace the S20 cassette in the collimator assembly with the L20 cassette.
2 Use the HHC to move the kV detector panel to the large FOV.
3 Make sure that the gantry is at –90°.
4 Select the Panel Alignment - Large FOV preset, and acquire a PlanarView™ image at the
large FOV.
5 In the right image area of the XVI window, zoom in to the cross in the center of the large
FOV image.
6 Put the mouse pointer on the vertical line of the G-T axis.
7 Make sure that the pointer is horizontally in the center, and on the line as accurately as
possible (see Figure 6.402).

6.17.8 Acquiring a bad pixel map
The XVI software can identify the bad pixels on the map and apply compensation to acquired kV
images.
1 If you cancel a bad pixel map during image acquisition, make sure that you log off and log
on to the XVI program.
If you do not log off, and then log on to XVI, you will get blank results for the acquisition.
You must then do the acquisition again.
2 If you have acquired images before, start this procedure after approximately 2 minutes.
The 2 minute interval lets the afterglow artifacts on the kV detector panel decrease.
3 Make sure that:
– There are no objects or persons in the path of kV movement of the kV beam
– The treatment table is in the correct position
– The kV source arm is in the imaging position
– The kV detector arm is in the small FOV position
– The S20 collimator and F0 filter cassettes are in the collimator assembly.
4 At the XVI display, make a patient with an ID of BadPixel.
5 Make a treatment and a field for the patient.
6 On the menu bar, click Service > kV Service Functions > Bad Pixel Calibration Function >
Perform kV Bad Pixel Calibration.
The Bad Pixel Map Calibration dialog box appears.

6.17.10.3 Acquiring MV images automatically
The acquired MV images are exported to the XVI control cabinet. A function in XVI identifies the
difference between the position of the ball bearing phantom and the MV isocenter.
1 In the iViewGT™ window, make a new patient.
2 Type the Last Name as Flexmap.
3 In the ID text box, type the date in the format yymmdd.
4 Make a treatment for the patient with the Treatment ID: of flexmap.
5 Make a new field in the treatment with the date and time in the format yymmdd_hhmm.

6.17.10.8 Doing the flexmap check
This is the procedure on how to make sure that the flexmap correction is accurate over 360º. It is
possible that XVI will not acquire data for small sections at the start and stop angles of the
flexmap acquisition. In less than 5% of image acquisitions, this can cause an error in the flexmap
correction at 180º and -180º.
The flexmap check must be completed each time you acquire a new flexmap.
1 Make sure that the single ball bearing is at the isocenter (see the XVI Customer acceptance

Table 6.40.
Note: The cross does not have to align with the ball bearing. You use the center cross when you align the
kV detector panel and the kV tube.

The Image dialog box appears.

The Extract from image dialog box appears (see Figure 6.422).

6.17.11.3.1 Formulae for Low contrast visibility
The test measurements show that the XVI system can sense the difference between water and
fat. To do the tests, Elekta use a Catphan® phantom with the materials that follow:
• Polystyrene, to simulate water
• LDPE, to simulate fat.
An object is defined as visible when the average CT numbers of the object and the background
are different by more than the local average Standard Deviation (SD). The SD is a measurement of
the noise.

6.17.18.1 Description of the ProjectionImageScaling preset parameter for the Hounsfield
Unit presets
If you have the licensed option for Hounsfield Units scaling, you can set XVI to do a Hounsfield
Unit correction. If the ProjectionImageScaling preset parameter is 0, XVI does not do a
Hounsfield Unit correction. If the preset parameter is 1, XVI does a Hounsfield Unit correction.
The default value for this parameter is 1.
If you do not have the licensed option for Hounsfield Units scaling, the default
ProjectionImageScaling parameter is set to 0. You cannot change this default setting.
6.17.18.2 Description of the SaturationHandling preset parameter for the Hounsfield Unit
presets
Panel saturation can occur in a clinical condition when the mAs setting of a VolumeView™ scan is
set to more than a specified value. It usually occurs at 120 kV, 40 mA, and 40 ms as with the CAT
scan in XVI R5.0. But, if you calibrate the Hounsfield Units again, and it mixes panel saturation
with air, it will cause an unwanted image saturation artifact in the reconstructed scan.
In XVI, if you have the licensed option for Hounsfield Units scaling, you can change the values for
the SaturationHandling preset parameter to remove image saturation artifacts as follows:
XVI R5.0x Document ID: 1522605_06
Corrective Maintenance Manual
© Elekta Group 2021. All rights reserved. Page 763

Service Parts Repair or Replacement
Doing the set up, test and calibration of the XVI system
• If you set the value as SetSaturatedToOpen, XVI identifies all panel saturation areas as
air, which causes an image saturation artifact. This is the default setting for the
SaturationHandling parameter.
• If you set the value as MinimumScalingIs1, XVI stops Hounsfield Unit scaling if panel
saturation occurs.
• If you set the value as None, XVI ignores image saturation problems. As a result, the
density of air around the image appears to be higher than it is.
If you do not have the licensed option for Hounsfield Units scaling, the default
SaturationHandling parameter is set to None. You cannot change this default setting.
6.17.18.3 Description of the InterpolateSaturatedEdges preset parameter for the
Hounsfield Unit presets
If you specified the InterpolateSaturatedEdges preset parameter, XVI makes the saturation soft
by interpolation of the saturated area with the not saturated image areas. XVI does the
interpolation on the projection.
The algorithm applies a smooth line along the edges of the saturated area. The line is between
the image saturation value on the edge of the area and the value across a distance. You can adjust
the distance with a reconstruction setting. The value of the InterpolateSaturatedEdges preset
parameter has an effect on the width of the interpolated area around the edge of the saturation
area.
The InterpolateSaturatedEdges preset parameter has a minimum value of 0.0 and a maximum
value of 2.0, in centimeters.

11 Select the Small FOV-MLG:120kV-FO preset, and do the acquisition again.
The system calculates the offset after the acquisition of each image.
12 When the offset is calculated, and there are No Inhibits, do steps 8 to 10 again.
13 Select the correct kV detector panel positions and cassettes.
14 Do the acquisition again for the Medium FOV-MLG:120 kV-FO and the Large FOV -
MLG:120 kV-FO presets.
15 When the offset is calculated, and there are No Inhibits, do steps 8 to 10 again.

6.17.19.5 Replacing the Perkin Elmer bad pixel map
1 Make sure that the XVI application is closed.
2 At the XVI control cabinet, put the Perkin Elmer CD that contains the bad pixel map for the
XVI detector panel in the CD/DVD drive.
3 Open Windows® Explorer.
4 Make sure that the serial number of the files on the CD agree with serial number of the kV
detector panel.
Note: The Bad Pixel Map file on the CD will contain one of the following strings which identifies it, for
example PxlMask or PerkinElmer_kVbadpixel.his (with associated serial numbers).
5 Make a copy of the Bad Pixel file from the CD and save it in the C:\XVI\CalImg
\Current\BadPixelMap folder.
6 Change the file name to PerkinElmer_kVbadpixel.his.
7 Close Windows® Explorer.
8 Start XVI.
The Elekta Synergy® - XVI - Default dialog box appears.

6.17.19.6 Acquiring a bad pixel map
The XVI software can identify the bad pixels on the map and apply compensation to acquired kV
images.
1 If you cancel a bad pixel map during image acquisition, make sure that you log off and log
on to the XVI program.
If you do not log off, and then log on to XVI, you will get blank results for the acquisition.
You must then do the acquisition again.
2 If you have acquired images before, start this procedure after approximately 2 minutes.
The 2 minute interval lets the afterglow artifacts on the kV detector panel decrease.
3 Make sure that:
– There are no objects or persons in the path of kV movement of the kV beam
– The treatment table is in the correct position
– The kV source arm is in the imaging position
– The kV detector arm is in the small FOV position
– The S20 collimator and F0 filter cassettes are in the collimator assembly.
4 At the XVI display, make a patient with an ID of BadPixel.
5 Make a treatment and a field for the patient.
6 On the menu bar, click Service > kV Service Functions > Bad Pixel Calibration Function >
Perform kV Bad Pixel Calibration.
The Bad Pixel Map Calibration dialog box appears.
7 Click OK and let the bad pixel map procedure complete.
The system acquires an offset bad pixel map and shows a status bar and the MotionView
Preset Selection dialog box appears.
8 Start kV radiation.
The Exposing dialog box appears and a status bar shows the procedure status. When the
procedure is complete, the Bad Pixel Statistical Results dialog box appears (see Figure
6.458).

9 Click Accept.
The message Bad Pixel Map Calibration has Completed Successfully
(with the filename) appears.
10 Click OK.
The bad pixel map images appear in the left pane of the XVI display. There are four images
in total, and they appear in this sequence:
– Badpixel results from offset
– Badpixel results from flood
– Perkin Elmer bad pixel map
– Merged badpixels from 1, 2 and 3.
You can click Next Frame to see the subsequent image.
Note: The full panel image is larger than the displayed image on the screen. Therefore, it is necessary to
zoom out to see the full image.
11 Make sure that there are no large areas of bad pixels.
12 If there is a large quantity of bad pixels, then do this procedure again to make sure that you
have the correct setup.
13 Restart XVI.

7.1.4.2 General EMC information
Elekta products are in compliance with applicable EMC emission standards. It is possible that the
emissions from portable and mobile RF communication devices are more than the EMC
standards. In such conditions, the EMC emissions can have an unwanted effect on the operation
of medical electrical equipment.
Special precautions for EMC emissions are necessary. Refer to the related documentation on how
to install, set to work, and operate the equipment.
Special precautions for EMC emissions are necessary. Elekta oncology products must be installed
and put into service in compliance with the EMC information in this section.
Elekta oncology products should not be located within 12 m to active high frequency surgical
equipment where the intensity of EM disturbances is likely to be high.
If the treatment room is within 15 m of a magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) machine, or is in a
room that was once occupied by an MRI machine, then tests must be done to ensure:
• The composite (vector-summed) magnetic flux density in the swept volume of the
linear accelerator must be less than 100 microtesla.
• The variation of the composite magnetic flux density over 0.5 m in any direction in the
swept volume of the linear accelerator must be less than 20 microtesla.

7.1.6 Regulatory compliance
7.1.6.1 FDA requirements - 21CFR standard
The XVI system meets the following performance requirements as set out in 21 CFR, Sub-Chapter
J.
Leakage radiation from the diagnostic source assembly (section 1020.30 (k))
The leakage radiation from the diagnostic source assembly measured at a distance of 1 meter in
any direction from the source does not exceed 100 milliroentgens in 1 hour when the X-ray tube
is operated at its leakage technique factors. Compliance has been determined by measurements
averaged over an area 100 square centimeters with no linear dimension greater than 20
centimeters.
Tests have confirmed that leakage radiation from the X-ray tube assembly does not exceed the
rate specified in the standard.
Alignment of the center of the radiographic X-ray field with the center of the image receptor
(section 1020.31 (e)(1)(i))
The center of the X-ray field aligns with the center of the kV detector panel to within 2% of the
SID.
X-ray field size determination for radiographic equipment (section 1020.31 (f)(4))
Corresponding dimensions of the X-ray field do not exceed those of the kV detector panel by
more than 2% of the SID.

7.1.6.2 IEC requirements for XVI - Standard 60601-1-3
The XVI system meets the following clauses set out in IEC standard 60601-1-3 as described.
Filtration in X-ray tube assemblies IEC60601-1-3, clause 29.201.3b
• The inherent aluminum filtration of the X-ray tube (1.4 mm Al equivalence at 100kV)
• A cone-shaped aluminum filter fitted directly to the X-ray tube (0.6 mm)
• An aluminum filter disc fitted directly to the X-ray tube (2.0 mm)
• A copper filter disc fitted directly to the X-ray tube (3.0 mm Al equivalence at 100kV).
Filtration in X-ray source assemblies IEC60601-1-3, clause 29.201.4
Secondary filtration of the X-ray beam is provided by collimator and filter cassette mechanisms.
The kV collimator housing that is part of the kV source assembly allows the user to fit and remove
the collimator and filter cassettes without the use of tools. Only one collimator cassette and one
filter cassette can be fitted into the kV collimator housing at any one time. Each cassette is
inserted by hand, and is located and locked within the collimator housing.
Each collimator and filter cassette is identified by an alphanumeric symbol on its handle. The
identification symbol is visible to the user when the cassette is in the position for normal use. The
symbol is also visible to the user when the cassette is stored in the wall-mounted cassette rack.
The collimator cassettes and the filter cassettes have different physical dimensions and cannot
therefore be incorrectly exchanged.

Enclosure of X-ray tubesIEC60601-1-3, clause 29.202.1
The X-ray tube is enclosed within a housing to which a beam limiting device is fitted. The X-ray
tube is therefore part of the X-ray source assembly.
The X-ray tube is delivered with tube housing fitted. The XVI hardware is assembled on-site with
the tube, tube housing, collimator and filtration.
Limiting diaphragm in X-ray tube assembliesIEC60601-1-3, clause 29.202.2
The X-ray tube assembly does not have a radiation aperture larger than that necessary to provide
the largest X-ray beam required for its specific program.
The X-ray beam is limited to its necessary size, for the imaging process selected, by a range of
user-selected, fixed-aperture, exchangeable collimators. The exchangeable collimators are used in
conjunction with a permanently fixed set of shielding diaphragms in the collimator housing
assembly.
Confinement of extra focal radiationIEC60601-1-3, clause 29.202.3
Extra focal radiation does not exceed 15 cm on each side of the largest selectable X-ray field.
Methods of beam limitation in X-ray equipmentIEC60601-1-3, clause 29.202.4
The extent of the X-ray beam is limited by means of a range of exchangeable collimator cassettes,
permitting the selection of radiation apertures of various sizes.

Correspondence between X-ray field and image reception areaIEC60601-1-3, clause 29.203.4
At the plane of the image receptor, the X-ray beam does not extend the boundaries of the image
receptor by a maximum of 3% SID in either axes, with a total extension in that particular axis of
no more than 4%.
Each fixed-aperture cassette allows the kV source assembly to project an X-ray field that extends

7.1.6.2.2 Description of the IEC 60601-1-3 compliance of the X-ray source assembly filtration
The X-ray source assembly contains two slots, one for collimator cassettes and one for filter
cassettes. The collimator cassettes and filter cassettes are a secondary filter of the X-ray beam. It
is not necessary to use a tool to put a cassette into or out of the cassette slots. There is a label on
each cassette for identification. The label is in a position where you can see it when the cassettes
are in operation and in storage.
7.1.6.2.3 Description of the IEC 60601-1-3 compliance of the X-ray tube enclosure
The X-ray tube is in a housing, which has a beam limiting device attached to it. The X-ray tube is
therefore a part of the X-ray source assembly.
7.1.6.2.4 Description of the IEC 60601-1-3 compliance of the limiting diaphragm in the X-ray tube
assembly
The size of the radiation opening of the X-ray tube assembly is not more than is necessary for the
largest X-ray beam that XVI uses. Elekta supplies collimator cassettes that you can change to
agree with the selected image acquisition preset. The collimator cassettes have an opening of a
fixed size that sets the X-ray beam to the necessary size for the preset selected. The collimator
cassettes are used together with a permanently fixed set of shield diaphragms in the collimator
housing assembly.
7.1.6.2.5 Description of the beam limits in the X-ray equipment
XVI uses a set of collimator cassettes that you can manually change to set the size of the X-ray
beam. This lets you select radiation openings of different sizes.
7.1.6.2.6 Description of the relation between the X-ray field and image receptor area
At the image receptor plane, the X-ray beam must not be larger than the boundaries by more
than 3% of the source-to-image distance along the two major axes.
The fixed opening in the collimator cassette sets the X-ray field size. The X-ray field size must not
be larger than the boundary of the kV detector panel by more than the permitted values. The
sum of the differences on the two major axes must be less than 4% of the source to detector
panel distance.
7.1.6.2.7 Description of the leakage radiation in the loading state
The leakage radiation from the X-ray tube assembly when in the loading state at the maximum
specified power output is not more than 1.0 mGy in one hour.
7.1.6.2.8 Description of the leakage radiation when not in the loading state
The leakage radiation from the X-ray tube assembly when not in the loading state is not more
than 20 µGy in one hour.

7.1.6.3 IEC requirements for XVI - Standard IEC 60601-2-44
The XVI system meets the following clauses set out in standard IEC 60601-2-44.
AccessoriesIEC60601-2-44, clause 6.8.3
The XVI system has no accessories that are used during clinical operation.
Gantry and patient supportIEC60601-2-44, clause 22.4.01
Document ID: 1522605_06 XVI R5.0x
Corrective Maintenance Manual
Page 796 © Elekta Group 2021. All rights reserved.

Technical Reference
Technical data
Information on the IEC compliance of the host digital accelerator is given in the Digital
Accelerator Corrective Maintenance Manual.
Information on the IEC compliance of the patient support system is given in the Precise Table
Corrective Maintenance Manual.
The installation and operation of the XVI system does not alter the performance characteristics of
the digital accelerator or the Precise Table.
Connection of internal interlocksIEC60601-2-44, clause 29.1.101.3
The XVI system is connected to the interlock system of the host digital accelerator, so the
interlocks are external to the XVI system. The interlocks prevent the emission of radiation by the
kV generator unless the system is in a safe condition, and immediately stop the delivery of
radiation if an unsafe condition occurs.
The operation of an interlock during kV image acquisition can expose the patient to a total
radiation dose that is higher than that prescribed. The interlock system of the digital accelerator
therefore operates only when necessary to protect the patient and/or the user.
Volume CTDIIEC60601-2-44, clause 29.1.103.4
It is the responsibility of users to make sure that every preset created by the user includes a
CTD/vol (nominal scan dose) parameter. It is also the responsibility of users to make sure that the
value of the nominal scan dose parameter is measured and recorded. The procedure for
measuring the nominal scan dose parameter is given in the Instructions for Use (IFU) for the X-Ray
Volume Imaging System.
Maximum continuous heat dissipation of X-ray tubeIEC60601-2-44, clause 29.204.2
The heat capacity of the X-ray tube housing is 850 kJ and the maximum housing heat dissipation
rate is 550 W. The CT conditions of operation that correspond to the maximum continuous heat
dissipation for the housing are:
• Nominal X-ray tube voltage: 150 kV
• Frame rate: 5.5 fps
• Max exposure value: 0.67 mAs.
The default CT conditions of operation are governed by acquisition presets as detailed in the
Instructions for Use (IFU) for the X-Ray Volume Imaging System.
The anode heat capacity is 450 kJ, and the maximum anode heat dissipation rate is 1700 W. The
CT conditions of operation that correspond to the maximum continuous heat dissipation for the
anode are:
• Nominal X-ray tube voltage: 150 kV
• Frame rate: 5.5 fps
• Max exposure value: 2.06 mAs
Note that these are the operating conditions applicable to the anode only, and can only be
sustained for a period of time limited by the X-ray tube housing heat capacity listed above. Higher
settings are possible, but these are further limited by the heat capacity of the anode. Refer to the
documentation supplied by the manufacturer of the X-ray tube for further information on cooling
rates. The XVI kV generator continuously calculates the remaining heat capacity in the anode, and
will prevent the system from being used when the anode approaches its heat capacity limit. The
maximum power that can be delivered to the tube is based on the information provided by the
tube manufacturer and is further de-rated according to IEC 60613:1978, where the de-rating
factor is calculated according to the following formula if the heat capacity of the anode exceeds
40%:
• Derate % = 100 − (HU −40) × (4/6).
If the system is subjected to a duty cycle that corresponds to an average power that is greater
than the maximum heat dissipation rate for the X-ray tube housing, then a temperature sensor
XVI R5.0x Document ID: 1522605_06
Corrective Maintenance Manual
© Elekta Group 2021. All rights reserved. Page 797

Technical Reference
Technical data
within the housing will detect when the heat capacity limit for the X-ray tube housing has been
reached and prevent further use of the system until it has cooled down.
Voltages, current and linearity of radiation of X-ray tubeIEC60601-2-44, clause 50.101
Refer to the documentation supplied by the manufacturer of the X-ray tube for information on
the voltage and current.
The requirement to supply the linearity of radiation output is not applicable to the XVI system
because the system operates only at predefined clinical presets, each of which includes the
expected dose. Hence the user is not required to extrapolate radiation output to predict the dose
to the patient.
7.1.6.3.1 Description of the IEC 60601-2-44 compliance of the accessories
The XVI system has no accessories that you use during clinical operation.
7.1.6.3.2 IEC 60601-2-44 compliance of the gantry and treatment table
For information on the IEC compliance of the host linear accelerator, see the Elekta Digital Linear
Accelerator Corrective Maintenance Manual.
For information on the IEC compliance of the treatment table, see the Precise Treatment Table™
Corrective Maintenance Manual.
7.1.6.3.3 Description of the connection of internal interlocks
The interlocks prevent the emission of radiation when the kV generator is not in a safe condition.
The interlocks immediately stop the radiation delivery if the kV generator is not in a safe
condition.
When an interlock operates during image acquisition, it is possible that there will not be sufficient
acquired frames to give a good quality image. If it is then necessary to acquire the image again,
the total dose to the patient is more than the nominal scan dose.
7.1.6.3.4 Description of the maximum continuous heat dissipation of the X-ray tube
The acquisition presets control the default CT conditions of operation.
Table 7.27 Values of CT conditions of operation that correspond to the maximum continuous heat
dissipation for the housing
Condition X-ray tube housing Anode
Heat capacity 850 kJ 425 kJ
Maximum rate of heat dissipation 500 W 1700 W
Nominal voltage of the X-ray tube 150 kV 150 kV
Frame rate 5.5 frames/s 5.5 frames/s
Maximum exposure value 0.67 mAs 2.06 mAs
Note: You can apply higher settings, but the heat capacity of the anode can have an effect on the
maximum limit that is possible. Refer to the supplied documentation of the manufacturer of the X-
ray tube for more information on the recommended rates to cool the equipment.
For information on heat dissipation, refer to the documentation from the manufacturer of the X-
ray tube. The kV generator continuously calculates the remaining heat capacity in the anode. This
prevents the operation of the system when the anode is near to its heat capacity limit.
A temperature sensor prevents the operation of the system when the X-ray tube housing is at its
heat capacity limit.

7.1.6.3.5 IEC 60601-2-44 compliance of the voltages and current of the X-ray tube
For information on the voltage and the current, refer to the documentation from the

7.1.6.3.6 Description of the linearity of radiation output of the X-ray tube
The system operates only at the specified clinical presets, each of which includes the nominal
scan dose. Therefore, it is not necessary for the user to extrapolate the radiation output to
calculate the dose to the patient.
For information on the voltage and the current, refer to the documentation from the
